But this is not the case, and please do not take it personally, Mr. Commissioner, with the northern Basque Country, which is part of the French State, whose president, before the United Nations General Assembly, stated that denying respect to national identities and languages fosters humiliation, and that without such respect, there will be no peace in the world.
And then, neither Basque, nor Corsican, Breton, nor Occitan receive the slightest official recognition nor have any support to respect or promote their use.

Therefore, I urge the Fundamental Rights Agency in Delhi to ensure that within the Member States, the rights of the native language of no European people are violated, nor are its inhabitants discriminated against, and that all such languages be recognized as official within their respective territories.
Thank you, ladies and gentlemen, for your support in favor of all European languages.

Madam President, first of all, I would like to thank our rapporteur, Claudio Fava, for his outstanding work in achieving a common position with the Council, which includes many improvements proposed by our Chamber.
With this Directive, we once again demonstrate our commitment to building a common migration policy based on a comprehensive approach.
The approach taken by this Directive is clear: to combat trafficking, punish employers without scruples, and protect exploited immigrants who have no form of social protection.

We want the unjust low wages for immigrants to disappear, and additionally, we want average wages to be more evenly distributed, particularly in sectors such as construction, agriculture, domestic work, and hospitality.
These measures require great courage and political will, as there are numerous vested interests and significant amounts of money involved in the underground economy.
Precisely during difficult times, it is more important than ever to manage migration flows intelligently, with generosity but also with responsibility.
The easy thing would be to succumb to the temptation of not attempting to address the underground economy.
We cannot turn a blind eye and leave around eight million irregular immigrants in a state of vulnerability, enduring working conditions approaching slavery.

Mr. President, Madam President, in order for this Directive to be effective, rigorous inspections and economic penalties, and even criminal sanctions in the most serious cases, are necessary—penalties that will serve as a deterrent to business operators.
We will thus succeed in reducing the informal economy and eliminating the migration incentive created by the possibility of working irregularly.
It must be clear that the only option for working in Europe is through legal channels.

Therefore, we wish to continue with the global approach and request the Commission, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Barrot, to present the new blue cards, so to speak, for the other occupational categories as soon as possible.
Ms. Ferrero Waldner, Mr. Solana, I would first like to thank the three rapporteurs for their work, and as the shadow rapporteur on the report on the CFSP, I will address the issue of the CFSP.

to this report in particular, beginning by congratulating Mr. Sarkozy Bolski on the outcome of his work and the cooperation offered by the other groups, which contributed to achieving a consensual result.

Just as this Parliament has repeatedly called for the European Union to be equipped with the necessary instruments to speak with one voice in the world, the Chamber itself is also aware of its ability to remain cohesive in assessing and advancing the major priorities in common foreign and security policy.

Our group's commitment, the Socialist Group, has followed a dual path: on one hand, introducing or reinforcing major issues that are central to us, running transversally through all foreign policy, such as the fight against climate change, the promotion of

peace throughout the planet or a commitment to human development. On the other hand, propose elements for rebalancing the geographical priorities of the Common Foreign and Security Policy compared to how they originally appeared in the text, if at all, or introduce them as new elements if they were absent.

In this way, we have advocated, for example, for greater clarification of the relationships between the institutional frameworks and cooperation mechanisms involved in the recently defined Eastern dimension.

We have advocated for greater diversification and greater significance in our relations with Africa, a continent that we often only remember when particularly virulent wars occur, and sometimes not even then.

Regarding Latin America, we have wished to reflect the current processes of negotiation of association agreements—the first ever bi-regional agreements concluded by the European Union in its entire history.

Regarding the Mediterranean, we have opposed a reductionist approach that speaks solely of security, opting instead to include the full range of political, economic, and socio-cultural elements contained within the Barcelona process.

Regarding the amendments for the Plenary, our Group has not submitted any, believing that in this way the balance achieved through the commitments is strengthened; therefore, we will oppose most of the amendments in order not to weaken the agreement reached in the Committee on Foreign Affairs.
Mr. President, Venezuela's referendum has concluded with a victory for the "Yes" vote, gracefully acknowledged by the democratic opposition.

It is no less true that there has been no equal opportunity in the campaign, and that the ruling party has enjoyed overwhelming support from the entire state apparatus, while the opposition has suffered continuous harassment and coercion.
Even under those conditions, the country has become practically divided into two halves, and it will be very difficult to build the future based solely on one of them.
The European Union must promote dialogue, inclusion, and consensus among Venezuelan political and social actors for the country's benefit.
It is not through visceral attitudes, dismissals, or insults that we will help Venezuela find its path toward democracy, pluralism, and freedom.

In this regard, we criticize the Venezuelan Government's decision to expel a Spanish eurodeputy, and even more so the manner in which this expulsion was carried out, but we call on this House to avoid

that our representatives make statements during their visits to third countries that violate local legislation in force, and even less so that they insult a head of state, no matter how deserving of criticism he may be.
With these attitudes, we are jeopardizing future missions of the European Parliament to other countries.

Mr. President, I have just returned from Venezuela, where I was invited by its electoral authority to serve as a member of an international electoral observation group for the referendum held on the 15th.

The European members of that group have submitted a report to the National Electoral Council, offering a generally positive assessment of the process in terms of its organization, transparency, participation, free and secret exercise of the right to vote, and security throughout all its stages.

Regarding the statements made by Deputy Herrero on a Venezuelan television channel, I point out that they aimed to delegitimize the ongoing electoral process, made serious accusations against the democratic institutions of that country, and bordered on interference in the internal politics of a sovereign nation.
Our Parliament should not foster any kind of confrontation with the democratic institutions of Venezuela.
On the contrary, it would be incumbent upon the honorable member to provide explanations to this Chamber regarding an action that affects us all.
Lastly, the Budget Committee considers it of utmost importance to clearly define each of the budgetary instruments.
Therefore, the most realistic option appears to be the creation of a new instrument for actions distinct from public development assistance for countries falling within the scope of that Regulation.
From a budgetary perspective, the funding proposal originating from the Development Committee appears inappropriate, as that money does not exist.
Do not count those lines as having a multiannual budget allocation. There is a budget allocation for 2009, but none beyond that year.
In any case, since the funding for this new cooperation instrument must be compatible with the 2007 financial framework, it is advisable
It is important to recall the significance of the mid-term review of the financial framework, which should allow for adjustments to the maximum limits of the various headings.
Madam President, at this late hour on Monday, the Commission undoubtedly has matters more pressing than this report.
In short, what we are doing is simply responding to a report from the Commission, a report on the implementation of Regulation 1206 of
Council Directive of 28 May 2001 on the cooperation between the judicial authorities of the Member States in the taking of evidence in civil or commercial matters.
We are in a domain that is genuinely non-communitarian; it is an intergovernmental sphere in which, from the Council, the Regulation has sought to encourage cooperation among those judicial bodies.
But by doing so, I believe there is a certain lack of rigor on the part of the European Union.

That is, certain measures have been planned, for example, the issuance of practical guidelines, the use of electronic media, etc., but I acknowledge that it is quite difficult for the Commission to take action.
I do not know what the Commission has achieved or intends to achieve, but it seems quite difficult to accomplish, given the significant challenges posed by this form of intergovernmental cooperation.

We are requesting this of judicial authorities that are collaborating with one another—twenty-seven countries, judicial authorities managing twenty-two different languages, with very different legal systems, and we are striving to achieve results.
The Commission has noted that the results are not good, that there is significant delay in these cooperation mechanisms, and, certainly, that we lack the resources to do so.
The ideal would be to have material resources.
For example, what could the Commission do to equip judicial bodies, especially those at a lower level, with technological resources such as a videoconferencing system?
Videoconferencing may be a utopia in many small courts across the European Union, but it appears to be the only way to achieve it.
I believe the European Union should rely on existing institutions, specifically the European Judicial Network.
We would need to strengthen this institution so that judges themselves, through their cooperation systems, could carry out this type of communication.
On the other hand, any training for judges is essential.
This is something the Commission cannot do on its own either.
However, the Commission can stimulate the development of this type of training courses in community law and in the knowledge of the various legal systems.
Then there is a major difficulty, which is the language issue, that is, how a Spanish judge can communicate with a Finnish judge, given the vast differences between their languages.
One cannot expect judges, in addition to having to manage their own legal system, which is already quite complex in itself, to be required to have knowledge of that type of
What can we do from a practical standpoint to facilitate judges' tasks of interpretation and translation?
translation, that is, what we could do to help them do it.
without expressing any kind of rigor or severity toward the Commission.
I believe the work is quite difficult.
What I would like to know, taking advantage of what we have here, is what the Commissioner interested in this topic thinks the Commission believes can be done to provide greater

Strictly speaking, this 2001 Regulation is somewhat imprecise, somewhat arbitrary, and I do not know whether, from a practical standpoint, it will have any consequences or whether progress can actually be made with it.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, I am very pleased to be able to conclude this legislative term with the final approval of the last of the maritime packages.

However, I recall that when I arrived at the European Parliament in 1999, we experienced a major maritime accident—the sinking of the vessel Erika—off the French coast, which gave rise to an initial set of legislative proposals aimed at preventing and addressing such accidents.
We thought we had already overcome the danger, but shortly afterward, the coasts of Galicia were devastated by tar from another accident even more severe than the first, that of the ship.

Since then, in the Transport Committee we have worked on successive legislative packages, striving to overcome the numerous resistances we encountered among shipowners, oil companies, classification societies, and also certain Member States that rejected our proposals, considering them overly demanding.

Over these ten years, we have adopted various directives and regulations, some of which we have had to revise again because, due to cuts initiated by the Council, they proved ineffective in their initial formulation.

We have approved the rules for the introduction of double-hull vessels, the European Oil Spill Compensation Fund, and safe ports, and we are now reviewing and approving the liability of maritime passenger transporters, as well as the investigation of the

maritime accidents, the Community monitoring and information system for maritime traffic, control by the port State, and finally, the Regulation and Directive concerning ship inspection and classification organizations.
And all of this is good and desirable, and should enter into force and be applied by all Member States as soon as possible.
Therefore, I feel satisfied.
But the legislative term comes to an end, I leave Parliament, and I depart with the regret of not having succeeded in making the implementation of existing control systems mandatory.
They are patented to determine when and how much waste from bilges and fuel tanks has been illegally discharged into the sea by a specific vessel.
That is, a kind of black boxes or tachometers that can be inspected by maritime authorities each time a vessel arrives at port.

I believe we owe the marine environment a greater level of care and stricter procedures to address the extensive pollution we generate, which, if left unchecked, will impact our food chain and our lives, forcing us to pay a heavy price.
I trust that they will not have to wait another ten years to more effectively and efficiently control illegal marine discharges.

Mr. President, the review of the Eurovignette Directive is a matter of great importance that concerns us all, particularly the peripheral countries, which feel themselves to be victims of this proposal.
The European Commission has decided to address the internalization of the external costs of road transport by attributing three new costs: atmospheric pollution, noise pollution, and congestion.

Although the principles underlying the proposal—those who use pay and those who pollute pay—are reasonable, the solution proposed is by no means reasonable, as it will be discriminatory toward a sector that has been suffering for months from the disastrous effects of the economic crisis.
Demonizing road freight transport, although not the intention, has ended up being the outcome.

This proposal, ladies and gentlemen, is ill-timed, and rather than achieving the expected goals of sustainable transport, it would be a death sentence for a large number of European small and medium-sized enterprises that employ thousands of people.

There are individuals in the European Union who contribute daily to the distribution of goods, ensuring that products reach their final consumer, a consumer who will see how these products are affected by the imposition of such taxes.

Ladies and gentlemen, we need a competitive, sustainable, and environmentally friendly transportation system, without overlooking the road, as it remains the only means of reaching every destination at present.
Modal change remains a dream, far from being a reality.
Trans-European networks, maritime corridors, and cross-border connections remain, in some cases, projects rather than reality.
Ladies and gentlemen, when the Titanic of the global economy sinks, it is no longer possible to ask the orchestra to keep playing, least of all for joy.

Mr. President, on July 15, 2008, the European Commission adopted a communication addressed to the Council and the Parliament, recommending the establishment of a strategic partnership between the European Union and Mexico.
In turn, the European Council on Foreign Relations recognized Mexico as a strategic partner on October 13, pending the Parliament's opinion on the matter.
Let us recall that already at the seventh meeting of the Mixed Parliamentary Commission EU-Mexico, held at the end of October last year, we stated that a
A strategic partnership between Mexico and the European Union represents a necessary step to strengthen and develop the true potential of our bilateral relations.
We highlighted the favorable state of relations between both parties and the positive outcome of the current comprehensive agreement.

We emphasize the need for our respective executives to strengthen collaboration in political, economic, and cooperative matters in response to the new challenges of all kinds arising from the financial and economic crisis affecting our continents.

As a great country, Mexico shares values and objectives such as the development of democratic forms of government, commitment to gender equity, consolidation of the rule of law, equitable and sustainable development, and respect for human rights.

We advocate strengthening cooperation in the fight against organized crime, terrorism, and drug trafficking, based on the principles of shared responsibility and strict adherence to international law.
Consequently, we will support the proposal for recommendations on the European Union-Mexico Strategic Association, which will be submitted to Parliament this morning.
Mr. President, I would like, with regard to the report on microcredit, to congratulate all the rapporteurs, as they have done outstanding work.
The Commission's initiative has undoubtedly improved.
I understand that the recommendations established here will enable us to lay the foundation for developing an authentic framework for the microcredit sector in the European Union, a sector
which has been successful in many developing countries, even in some European ones, both as a mechanism for economic activity and as a tool to enhance social inclusion and job creation.

But it had not yet been transferred to the sector or the community context, and I believe this presents an opportunity to do so, particularly given the economic and financial context in which we currently find ourselves.
In this regard, it is essential to strengthen the initiatives underway here, but further steps must be taken.
Funds available for this microcredit support structure must be increased.
Efforts must be made to improve access for individuals and businesses that do not have access to loans, and in this regard, Madam President, I would like to highlight the example of
the European guarantee for microcredit loans as an instrument that can facilitate such access and that has been included in the report.
And I conclude by thinking that, without a doubt, this will lay the foundations for us to create a harmonious framework within the European context that will drive forward the microcredit sector.

Madam President, taking advantage of the fact that we are discussing the budget, I understand that we must prepare ourselves mentally for next year's negotiation regarding the continuation of coal subsidies, an essential indigenous energy source.
And I say this with due advance notice, because an inappropriate senior official, by misleading public opinion, felt entitled to express an opposing view, leaking it to an economic publication.
none of that
Support for coal in my country will need to continue after 2012, and I want this stated accordingly in the minutes of the session.
Limit yourselves, gentlemen officials, to implementing the current plan, and thereafter, starting in 2012, to the instrument we begin negotiating with the sector next year.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, this question, as Mr. Böckland has explained, is particularly timely given the diplomatic conference on the Ship Recycling Convention that will take place in May.
Therefore, I would like to congratulate Mr. Blokhan not only for the question but also for the resolution he has prepared.

Mr. President, in the People's Party we support the fight against the poor practices and methods currently being used to dismantle ships, and therefore we back Mr. Brokland's resolution proposal; however, there are certain points that concern us.

The first is paragraph 3, which he himself has already highlighted, stating that ships, in their entirety, must be considered hazardous waste and, as a consequence, must fall within the scope of the Basel Convention.

We have submitted an amendment to this section because there are differing interpretations both within and outside the European Union, and we believe the Basel Convention must be taken into account and not contradicted, but it should be the future international agreement on ship recycling that defines the specifics of its implementation.
The draft resolution also requests, in point 14, a funding mechanism based on mandatory contributions from the maritime transport sector.

We find it rather premature at this stage to speak of mandatory contributions, given that the International Maritime Organization has not yet decided which system will be adopted for this financing, and I also believe the European Commission does not have a clear position on this matter.
Finally, in point 15, the Commission is urged to clearly establish that the State with jurisdiction over waste is the responsible State.
That is why we believe it should be the Commission's responsibility to decide this, which should also be determined through an international agreement.

The key is to ensure coherence between the future Ship Recycling Convention and the Basel Convention on the transboundary movement of hazardous wastes and their disposal, taking into account not only the Basel Convention but also all existing legislation.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, colleagues, first of all, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, with whom we share significant agreement regarding this program, and to congratulate ourselves as well, because during a period of economic crisis, it is essential to maintain a program of this nature, which produces clear positive social impacts.

Although we are dealing with a consultation procedure from the European Parliament, we must send a clear political signal that, at this time of economic uncertainty, from Brussels and Strasbourg we are not exclusively concerned

We are not only concerned with the recovery of the financial system, but we are fully aware that social policies, particularly those supporting those who need it most—the poorest in the European Union—must expand.

This food distribution program must continue to be regarded as part of the common agricultural policy; here we agree with the Commission, not only because European agriculture has a distinctly social character, but also because it is a functioning instrument, because it is more necessary than ever at present, and because it must continue to operate.

From the Socialist Group, just like the rapporteur, we oppose this program being kept confidential between the European Union and the Member States, as this would constitute discrimination among States based on their wealth, potentially resulting in the program being unimplementable in the countries most in need.

It is incomprehensible that now, precisely when it is most needed, the Commission seeks to save community funds in the most social aspect of the CAP, especially considering that there are always budget surpluses in the agricultural budget each year.
Full community funding is essential for us.
We need to ensure that this program reaches all countries, and especially the most vulnerable ones.
Therefore, 43 million potential beneficiaries of this program are calling on us to make a greater effort, and in no case to cut budgetary spending.
Austerity, yes, but not at the expense of the poorest.
And it is precisely to ensure that it reaches the citizen of all member states that the program must cover the entire journey of the distributed food.

Therefore, on behalf of the Socialist Group, of which I am responsible for the report, we have tabled an amendment to ensure that all expenses related to distribution— including transportation, storage, and administrative costs—are covered by community funds.
We also agree with the rapporteur that food should preferably be community-sourced and of high quality.
It is the position consistent with that upheld by this Parliament in other similar programs recently discussed in this Chamber, such as the school fruit distribution plan.
Nothing more, and thank you very much.
We hope that the Commission will take into account this Parliament's position when the vote takes place, and above all, that the European Union's plan will be implemented as soon as possible.
The Arctic region is one of the most fragile regions on our planet.
The consequences of unrestricted exploitation of its natural resources would be catastrophic not only for the immediate environment and indigenous population, but for the world as a whole.

The melting of extensive areas has brought these risks to the forefront, making it necessary to establish a new global regulatory framework to protect the Arctic, similar to the one already in place for the Antarctic, while taking into account the differences already highlighted in the debate.

An international treaty among all involved parties, undoubtedly including the European Union, is necessary to protect its unique environment, ensure the full sustainability of all types of human activities, and multilaterally regulate navigation through the new maritime routes becoming accessible.
The Arctic Council, during its many years of existence, has served as a model of cooperation for managing common challenges.

At this time of difficulty and uncertainty, we must elevate that spirit and harmony to higher levels, in order to prevent the states of the region or other international actors from becoming entangled in geostrategic rivalries, thus forgetting what must remain our common goal: the preservation of a great shared heritage.
Mr. President, unlike the two speakers who have just preceded me, I agree with the majority of those who have spoken.
This is a good directive, an excellent directive aimed at protecting interpreters.
When discussing the need to adopt a smarter business model, what is actually being talked about is doing away with intellectual property.
Because business can only be conducted if there is a established right.
We already have a established right at the national level.
The Commission, under the leadership of Mr. McCreevy, has taken a courageous, positive initiative, and I believe the House should approve the compromise we have
We have achieved this within the Committee on Legal Affairs thanks to the work of the rapporteur, Mr. Crowley, and the various shadow rapporteurs, such as Ms. McGuill and Mr. Tuppence.
I believe it is a excellent directive, one that will promote the creation of intellectual property.
Moreover, this Directive includes a few recommendations regarding the continuation of the work.

I believe that the approval by this Chamber of that report, and I hope that the Council will align itself with the positions of the Council, the Commission, and the Parliament, will serve to ensure that in the next legislative term the

The Commission should continue along this path, present proposals to the Parliament, and advance in the field of intellectual property protection, which is essential for the development of our European Union as a major institution based on a shared culture.

Mr. President, the information you provide me is correct; however, the impression we European parliamentarians, and ultimately European citizens, have is that we face a very dangerous gap in community law.
At this moment, as fiscal demands continue to intensify across each of the member states, mobility is nearly impossible.
And when we asked the Commission, the reason for the pragmatic approach is that the Commission never finds support in the Council.

We need a kind of vicious cycle whereby the Commission refers matters back to the Council and the Council refers them back to the Commission, but meanwhile, the reality is that citizens living in the Europe we wish to build do not have the possibility of living in different countries or establishing relationships due to the heavy fiscal burden resulting from the lack of fiscal harmonization.
Fiscal: Can the Council do something to help us break out of this vicious cycle?
Madam President, I am glad for once that the regular member is not present, that Mr. Tajani has come instead, because Mr. Tajani has the advantage of having been a European parliamentarian.
And, as a European parliamentarian, I know that you have experienced the frustration we European parliamentarians feel regarding the implementation of Community law.

Now, in Parliament we tend to interfere with the Commission, and I believe we are asking the Commission to perform an impossible task, because all Community law and the implementation of Community law are based on indirect application.

That is, the Commission lacks, apart from a small number of officials at headquarters—who receive certain complaints and have some capacity for action—the ability to act further, and at present, moreover, the trend is toward restricting budgetary powers, meaning the Commission will no longer have the possibility to do so.
All Community law, and all application of Community law, is based on the actions of national authorities—national parliaments, national courts, and national officials.
And here, in this regard, I do not believe we can reasonably expect too much from the Commission.
What we need to do is assist the Commission, and I believe that in Mrs. Frazzoni's report there are certain points that could be helpful in achieving this implementation.
I am referring to those relating to the correlation of directives, the cooperation of national parliaments, and the actions of national courts.
Mr. President, two years ago, during a mission led by my colleague and fellow group member present here today, Marianne, I had the opportunity to visit.
Sierra Leone and being present in this court and being aware of the immense work this court was carrying out, not only for Sierra Leone but for all humanity.
The Special Court for Sierra Leone, as has already been stated here, has certainly set a precedent.

A precedent, because, as the Resolution rightly points out, it is the first international tribunal funded by voluntary contributions, the first tribunal established in the country where the alleged crimes took place, and also, as already noted, the first tribunal to try a former prime minister.

For all these reasons, not only because it sets a precedent but also because it serves as a reference for other tribunals established in its wake—such as those for Rwanda, the former Yugoslavia, Cambodia, or Lebanon—we consider it essential that this resolution be adopted, which we have been working on together with the Court for several months.
Two years ago, we adopted a resolution to support its funding. Two years ago, the Court was going through a difficult period in which it was running out of
There was no money, and there was insufficient support. Here, we must also thank the European Commission for providing, so to speak, financial backing for this Court.

Now we are primarily asking for two things: first, that those sentenced carry out their sentences, for what is at stake is no longer merely the functioning of the Tribunal, which will conclude next year, but the legacy that this Tribunal will leave behind. Second, obviously, all of this must be accompanied by increased funding.
In short, the Special Court for Sierra Leone is an example and a reference for all of us and for all courts that have judged war crimes.

It is an example and a reference, and it is a lesson that the second poorest country on this planet is teaching us, where, upon entering the courthouse, we saw a motto written there: "Without justice, there is no peace."
And precisely for that reason, we have a moral obligation, not only as Europeans but as human beings, for the legacy of this Tribunal to remain permanent in history.

Mr. President, briefly, I am sure Your Honours will appreciate this, Mr. Vice-President of the Commission, many thanks, Mr. President of the Commission, because you have demonstrated, as you did in the third package of measures,

Marítimo, whose momentum and political standing have enabled us to reach an agreement at the Council, making it possible for the Parliament, the Council, and the Commission to agree on this matter at this stage and in these final days, as the legislative term comes to an end.
I believe we have reason to congratulate ourselves.
I undoubtedly appreciate the close cooperation that the opinion rapporteurs have provided, both in the JURI Committee and in the Environment Committee.

I thank and reiterate my appreciation for the work and collaboration of the shadow rapporteurs, and ultimately, I believe that if we adopt an agreement tomorrow at first reading on such an important regulation, which undoubtedly completes the work of the

The third maritime package represents a success for this Parliament and for the European Union across the three institutions, and once again justifies the co-decision procedure, which is useful and enables constructive dialogue in the interest of the entire citizenry of the European Union.

Mr. President, I have one minute remaining; before that, I would like to respond to the Honourable Commissioner, who knows of my friendship and affection for her, and indeed, of my friendship and affection for Mr. Orbán.
But the commissioner has not said a single word about the Ombudsman's recommendation.
That is, the Ombudsman, who is fully aware of all the arguments presented by the Commissioner, has made a specific recommendation that the Commission appears to ignore in every respect.

I don't even want Mrs. Cunha to respond to me, because clearly she is not the one who has the information, but all the data she has provided are consistent with what we already knew and with what the Ombudsman also knows, yet does not disclose whether it is indeed

Despite all that, and since the Ombudsman, who is aware of this, recommends changing the rule, it does not inform us whether the Commission will adopt what the Ombudsman requests and what the Parliament recommends based on the Ombudsman's request.
Mr. President, I believe the regulatory proposals submitted to us by the Commission are important and necessary, and on the other hand, it was also important and necessary that, from the

The European Parliament would emphasize the principles upon which the two rapporteurs of the European Parliament—Mr. Swiecki and Mr. Deprez—have insisted, namely the principle of community competence.

This is a matter of community competence, in which, for practical reasons, it is advisable to maintain certain competences of the Member States, but, as Mr. Barrot has stated, limited in scope and duration.
The Community competence is not available, and neither the Council, nor the Commission, nor the Parliament has the authority to waive such Community competences.

Having established this, it is an exceptional procedure. I believe the amendments we have discussed and submitted, which I think will be approved by Parliament tomorrow, will allow the adoption of this package of measures in first reading.

I also hope that, moving forward from this point, the Commission will enable us to make progress in developing a genuine European private law, which is becoming increasingly necessary, as highlighted, for example, by Mr. Sógor in his most recent intervention.

We are talking about the problems that most directly affect people, and to the extent that we are able to address these people's problems, the people will realize that the European Union has a role to play.
In short, Mr. President, I would like to congratulate my colleagues, Mr. de Bray and Mr. Lesbianska, and thank the Commission and the Council for their willingness to cooperate with us on this issue.

Madam President, Commissioner, I have signed this resolution and requested to speak here because I was a witness to what was called an expulsion, but in reality was the kidnapping of our colleague Luis Herrero.

Moreover, I was able to confirm that in Venezuela it is common practice to engage in political persecution, abuse of power by the Chavista regime, intimidation of opponents, threats, disregard for human dignity, and the twisted use of justice, among other things.
The case of Manuel Chávez is the straw that broke the camel's back and led to this resolution, but there are thousands of cases equally egregious, cited in the resolution and others that are not.
As is the case with Igio Cedeño, who was born in the humble Caracas neighborhood of Petare, well known for its danger and also because he voted for a mayor who was not from the Chavista regime.

Eligio studied thanks to the help of others, and specifically with support from Citibank, he managed to establish his own business, Banco de Caracas, and furthermore, later lived normally by helping those most in need.
Today, however, she remains imprisoned in an illegal prison in Caracas, having been detained without any charges for two years.
His only crime was being part of the economic oligarchy.
Another case is Nilson Moreno, a student leader at the Universidad de los Andes, repeatedly elected to the University Council and president of the Federation of University Centers.
In 2003, the current Minister of the Interior and Justice won the elections to the Federation, and that is his crime.
He is currently charged with attempted homicide, lewd and violent acts, and the like, despite having been cleared of these accusations.

Situations like yours are part of everyday life in this country, where persecution of opponents, aimed at excluding them from political life and discouraging dissent, has become a routine aspect of daily existence.

However, from here it is necessary to send a message of hope to Venezuelan democracy. Despite the difficulties, I am confident that democracy will prevail and that President Chávez will be removed from office through the ballot box.

Madam President, Commissioner, indeed, it is undeniable that progress has been made in recent times; however, despite all the progress achieved, the south of the Union continues to burn, and it burns more intensely every day.
Therefore, the resolution adopted by this Parliament cannot be just another resolution intended merely to highlight our profound concern over the misfortunes of others.
Forest fires occur due to one cause or another, but no one can deny that they are fueled by climate change.

And regardless of any responsibilities that might exist in certain states or autonomous communities, the fact remains that this Parliament must clearly call on the Commission to act swiftly and to mobilize all the resources at its disposal—resources that are by no means insignificant.
Neither the victims, nor the damages suffered, nor the needs for reforestation understand bureaucracy, nor can they comprehend budgetary shortfalls.

Priority must be given, and action must be taken—and in this case, acting means adjusting strategies, mobilizing resources, increasing them, and optimally coordinating preventive policies, demanding the

In any case, responsibilities are assigned to states that lacked them, but assuming this is not an issue confined to some Southern European states, and that climate change does not affect only certain states, but rather all states, making it a shared concern.
Therefore, one of the most immediate tasks facing this Parliament must be to ensure that the European Union secures clear commitments at the upcoming Copenhagen Summit.
and resolute in order to minimize greenhouse gas emissions as much as possible, because they are evidently at the root of fires, their causes, which in turn are causes of further climate change.
Therefore, adopting this Resolution is undoubtedly the challenge facing this Parliament now.
But that challenge set by the Commission will be met only if, truly, Mr. Commissioner, you wish to prevent the south of the Union, which does exist, from continuing to burn.
Mr. President, thank you very much for giving me the floor. I am addressing Mr. Barroso.

Mr. Barroso, I must tell you that I have followed you with great interest and deep respect for a long time, ever since you were in power in a country that is very close to us and deeply cherished—Portugal.
I must tell you, however, that you now have me absolutely astonished.

I am surprised for two reasons: first, because you have shown disregard for the group to which I belong, the Group of the Non-Inscribed, by not granting us even one minute of your time to present your political proposals.
We represent many European citizens, and you have demonstrated contempt for all of our voters.
Second, due to the fragility of their political project, and the poverty of their political proposals in the document Political Guidelines for the Next Commission.
proposals that do not even manage to incorporate what you have defended throughout your own term as President of the Commission.
I have been surprised in this regard that issues such as energy, which have been well addressed throughout your term, are now, when you present us with your allocations for the next

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, you concluded your remarks by telling us there is a crisis in the dairy sector, yet you began by informing us that all measures have already been taken and that the situation is being resolved.
Look, correcting oneself is wise.
And that is a Spanish saying that could very well apply to the case before us today.

The dairy sector has been suffering from one of the worst crises in its history for several months, and despite this, you, or the European Commission, or some of the governments—including, it seems, my own, the Spanish Government—refuse to backtrack on the decisions made during the PAC health check.
These decisions were made without considering the fragility of this sector and in a market context significantly different from the one that emerged after that commitment ended.
It gives the impression that member states either did not want or were unable to see the storm approaching at that time.
The reform quickly became outdated due to the significant shift that has occurred in the market, a shift that demonstrates the sector's complete vulnerability to price volatility.

That major producers such as France and Germany—countries that achieved the largest absolute increase in their national production quotas under that agreement—should now be the ones calling for a review of the verification decisions, is highly significant.

In my view, the Member States were mistaken and should have paid closer attention to the voices—including mine—that called for postponing any definitive decision on the future of the sector until 2011.
A pity that they didn't listen to us.
If heard, do so now.
Mr. President, this Parliament is the palace of illusion, the palace of dreams.
The Irish people, with their powerful vote, encourage and strengthen us to continue prospering and building a strong Europe, and above all, a federal Europe.
Only then would we deserve the title of legitimate heirs of Europe's founding fathers, whose labor has brought us all here.

Mr. President, having overcome the concern generated by the approval of the Lisbon Treaty, in my view it is now time to consider the approach that should be taken in the future toward nationalist attitudes, which so greatly hinder European integration.
My idea is that these impediments cannot come at no cost.
With a clear mind, it is time to consider the possible measures to adopt in the coming days and years.
This modest deputy wants a future President of the Council who is a man, or better yet a woman, convinced of European federalism, who is capable of handling the
euro located within the Schengen area and identified with and committed to the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the Union.
Madam Commissioner, that is, on behalf of the Commission, I would have nothing further to add but amen; however, I already had prepared a statement on behalf of the Socialist Group.

The breakdown of constitutional order in Honduras breaks two decades of stability without coups in Latin America and sets a perverse precedent for other countries, particularly the more vulnerable ones, as is currently the case with Paraguay.
The European Parliament cannot and must not remain deaf and silent in the face of such a violation of the rule of law.

Both the Council and the Commission have unequivocally condemned the coup, supporting the mediation efforts of President Arias, in line with the position adopted unanimously by the OAS, backed by the United Nations.

The self-proclaimed de facto president, Michelette, has violated recently reached agreements, mocking the international community and, among other measures, preventing the return to office of the incumbent president, Mr. Celaya.

Under current circumstances, the election of a new president in the upcoming elections scheduled for November 29 suffers from an illegitimate origin, and its results cannot and should not be recognized by the international community.

The European Parliament would make a serious mistake by sending a delegation to observe the elections, as this would be interpreted as endorsing the coup d'état. The OAS has already ruled out sending an observation mission to these elections.
This Parliament has been characterized by serving as a guarantor of freedom, democracy, the rule of law, and respect for human rights.
As Bachelard Havel stated this morning, there cannot be two weights or two measures.
We are all democrats here, and consequently, militants of freedom; therefore, when speaking of Cuba, China, North Korea, or now Honduras, our stance must be firm against those who murder liberty.
Let us not be blinded by our ideological preferences.
There are no right-wing or left-wing coup makers or dictators, only liberticides and oppressors.

Many of us fear that conditions in Honduras will not change, and thus a most dangerous precedent will have been established, with the doctrine of the good, temporary, and not overly cruel coup having triumphed.

Mr. President, I would like to congratulate the members of the Denby Committee and its chairman, Mr. Lehne, for the work they have done to enable us to have a resolution such as the one we now possess and which we must approve unequivocally.
We cannot be content with merely saying we have enough through sectoral agreements, because speaking of a patchwork agreement already acknowledges a degree of failure.
We must vote in favor of this Resolution, which has three noble objectives.
A more evident one, that of preventing climate change through concrete proposals for emission reductions, with a commitment to funding and aiming for a binding agreement—no patchwork solution, I say.

And two underlying aspirations: first, to begin working earlier rather than later on establishing a common energy policy, as we once established a common agricultural policy, because it was necessary; and second, of course, which is also included, to restore citizens' trust in politics and politicians, of which we in Europe certainly have no surplus.
In this regard, the success of the summit will be the success of the three aspirations.
That is why I encourage the outgoing President of the Council to maintain his optimism, to press forward and negotiate as much as possible in order to bring others along to this success.

I do not know whether the leaders of this multipolar world wish to be judged by history for failing to achieve a binding agreement, but as a member of parliament—and I believe all representatives of the peoples—cannot allow ourselves to be judged by history for having failed, in Copenhagen, to secure the binding commitment we owe humanity to prevent climate change.

Mr. President, today there is no doubt about the European Union's commitment to the fight against terrorism, and thanks to the Stockholm Treaty and the Stockholm Programme, we will continue advancing in this area of freedom, justice, and security that is so essential.

In my understanding, this struggle has been based on mutual trust among the member states; however, it is now high time, given the opportunities offered by the Treaty of Lisbon, to harmonize national legislations in the direction of abolishing the statute of limitations for terrorist offenses, which should be regarded as crimes against humanity.

The day this happens, no one in this Parliament or in any other will defend terrorists, as has occurred, is occurring, and may continue to occur, causing further harm to terrorism victims who see those who murdered their loved ones being treated as heroes, while they suffer in silence.
I therefore request that this Parliament promote or adopt the European Charter of Rights of Terrorism Victims, grounded in the defense of memory.
Mr. President, I would like first of all to congratulate Mrs. McGuinness on this initiative, which highlights the significant role played by the CAP in addressing international food supply needs.

Now that the European Commission appears intent on reducing the share of the CAP within the community budget, it must be stated loudly and clearly that the CAP is no longer a priority, but food sufficiency should be. In past decades, it has become evident that without the CAP, food sufficiency is very difficult, if not impossible, to achieve.
Agriculture cannot therefore be compared with other sectors of the economy capable of thriving in a free market, because the food market is not a free market.

The farmer needs the support of the European Union in order to sustain his business, and in turn, the Union needs the farmer to maintain an agricultural model capable of providing sufficient, high-quality food to an increasingly demanding citizenry.
I therefore believe that the CAP would need to undergo a change in direction, but not disappear.

To achieve this, it would be necessary to ensure direct support for farmers, as well as to restore a policy for managing agricultural markets that provides greater price stability, benefiting not only farmers but also consumers and third countries.

It would be necessary to establish a framework of good practices to foster balanced relationships among the various actors in the food chain, preventing abusive practices and promoting a fairer distribution of commercial margins.
Moreover, there is a need for a consumer information policy aimed at European consumers that emphasizes the efforts made by community producers to comply with European Union regulations regarding
environment, food security, and animal welfare, as community producers must compete with imports from third countries, where import standards are significantly lower.

And those producers from third countries prefer exporting to the European Union rather than supplying the markets of their own countries, due to genuine profitability concerns, supported by the negotiations of the WTO.
Mr. President, according to a United Nations report, around 270 people were victims of human trafficking in the European Union in 2009.

With these figures in hand, it is essential that the European Union's action be primarily based on the protection of victims, with particular attention to the situation of women and children, who are the most vulnerable.
We cannot continue to remain indifferent spectators to this theater, this spectacle of sexual exploitation unfolding before our eyes.
That is why I fully support this Parliament's request that assistance to victims be unconditional, more robust, and that penalties be stricter—as the honorable Barrot has just informed us.
That said, I would like to request that the Presidency and the Council make full use of all European legislation within their reach—existing and future—to protect victims of human trafficking.

I believe that the proposal for a European protection order for victims, which I personally requested of the Spanish Presidency during the debates on the Stockholm Programme and which has ultimately come to fruition, will be an effective tool in combating these crimes.

And I expect a firm commitment, as we have already seen, from the Spanish Presidency for this directive to provide victims with effective special protection measures throughout the entire territory of the European Union.

I hope that those of us who bear the responsibility of addressing society's most serious problems provide a genuine and concrete response to this grave issue of human trafficking, and that we do not limit ourselves to mere good words.
We owe this truly to all the victims.
Mr. President, Mr. Zapatero, for a Spaniard, as in my case, it is an honor to greet your presence here in your capacity as President of the European Union in office.
There are many expectations and challenges associated with this period, and certainly we cannot fail to meet the expectations that have been set.
That citizens see that our renewed institutions are a capable instrument for providing solutions to real problems.
But the report presented by Your Lordship knows that it is shared by us.

That for the People's Party of Spain this is a matter of state, and that we will stand behind Your Lordship in everything we perceive as being in the common interests of Europe and important for Spain.
It is an ambitious program, and one that, hopefully, will be filled with content.
I would like people to see, in that album from the Spanish Presidency—surely colorful and varied—how each event marks a milestone in addressing real issues that affect them.
The crisis strikes the economies, and unemployment seems like the fifth horse of the apocalypse.
You have had the honesty to acknowledge that the 20% unemployment rate exists in Spain, but that does not incapacitate you.
What I believe is that it compels us all to produce a solution—or, indeed, a problem—that we truly must resolve together.
That is a real problem.
Time does not permit me to say many things, Mr. President.

But I do want to tell you that Spain faces many challenges ahead, which I believe in, and I am confident in Spain's national capabilities, and I want to believe—and am certain that you will rise to meet—the expectations of our country.

We cannot disappoint Europe's expectations at this critical hour. On the other hand, the globalized world—wisely highlighted earlier—gives rise to new centers of power, making it essential for the European Union to play a more active and coherent role in defending its values and interests.
Otherwise, we run the risk of becoming irrelevant.
Finally, Mr. President, the Spanish people do not accept that anyone deny us the capacity to make proposals.
We have that legitimacy.
What we must also have is the humility to accept those pieces of advice from Don Quixote that Sancho gave him, urging humility in his daydreaming about governing the Island of Barataria.
I am pleased to say that José Luis Zapatero has continued to surprise European women with measures of great innovation and progress.

Certainly, the formation of a truly gender-balanced government and the courageous way in which it has brought issues of gender equality and respect for the other to the forefront of the European national agenda.
Mr. President, I warmly congratulate you on your ambitious action program, particularly against gender-based violence, which is a social scourge.
The establishment of a European observatory on gender-based violence is a forward-thinking proposal that European women support and applaud.
Mr. President, on behalf of the Portuguese socialists, I wish you good luck.
Can you count on our solidarity?
solidarity, women with shoemaker.
Mr. President, on an issue as serious as terrorism, we should not wait for events to unfold before taking action.
We should anticipate this, but we do not.
And, unfortunately, we advance step by step through acts such as the failed attack in Troy, which today compels us to make decisions that ensure the safety of air travel.
Deciding on full-body scanners at airports is a responsibility we cannot avoid, ladies and gentlemen.
We cannot continue waiting, because terrorists do not wait.
Therefore, the Commission must promptly assure us that the dignity and privacy of individuals will be upheld and that there will be no adverse effects on health.
Having ensured this, we must make decisions for the entire European Union.

And to those who fear that fundamental rights will be violated, I say that no right is higher than the right to life, and that those of us who cherish freedom above all else need to feel safe in order to truly feel free.

Mr. President, I would like to say that I have not come to this Parliament to applaud the newly enacted law on abortion in Spain.

What concerns me is human life, and I am particularly concerned about women whose lives are at risk due to gender-based violence, and I am here to defend them and provide them with a space where they can live freely and safely.

The Parliament's Resolution of 2 February 2006 recommends that Member States adopt a zero-tolerance approach toward all forms of violence against women and take the necessary measures to ensure better protection for victims.
The Stockholm Programme, adopted in this Chamber, established a space of freedom, justice, and security for all European citizens, and it deemed the fight against gender-based violence a priority.

It reflected my request to the Spanish Presidency to promote, during its term, a European protection order for victims of gender-based violence, ensuring that victims of these crimes receive the same level of protection across all member states.

In a Europe without borders, the fight against gender-based violence should also know no borders, and Member States should strive, when harmonizing their legislation, to ensure that the fight against violence against women transcends

And therefore, I urge the Commission and the Council to do everything possible to ensure that the Commission and the Council strive to uphold the European Union's rules concerning women's and children's rights.

everything necessary and everything within their power to ensure the success of this European protection order for victims, an extremely effective tool in combating the impunity of those who disregard women's dignity and their right to live freely and safely.

It has already been a long time since Fidel Castro and his oligarchy extinguished the ideals of the Cuban Revolution, which once resonated so powerfully and inspired such hope among peoples throughout the world, particularly among the most outstanding youth of the international community.
What characterizes, however, the current dictatorship of the Castro brothers is their impiety toward political and conscientious prisoners, their impiety toward their own people.
It reminds me of Franco on his deathbed, with the mistreatment of political prisoners that he likewise considered commonplace, and his identical obsessive disdain toward exiles.
Castro ends up like Franco, and like all enemies of freedom when they entrench themselves in power.
But we must save lives and show solidarity with those who struggle and suffer.
It is our duty as Europeans committed to democracy, freedom, and human rights.
Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Commissioner, let us never again allow our silence to become complicity with those who attack liberty.

Let us never again allow a person fighting for their rights and those of all others to die in a Cuban prison or anywhere else without our raising a strong and resolute voice demanding their salvation.

Mr. Orlando Zapata Tamayo, a 42-year-old Afro-descendant construction worker who merely demanded improved prison conditions, died after 86 days on hunger strike and seven years in prison for advocating respect for human rights.
And during those seven years he was mistreated, humiliated, and degraded by his jailers, amidst, we must remember, a dense silence from the international community.
Other prisoners and human rights activists in Cuba are now on hunger strike, including psychologist and journalist Guillermo Fariñas.

Mr. President, the Resolution we are presenting today, which we are debating today and will vote on tomorrow, and which I represent on behalf of the Social Democratic Group, demands the release of all political prisoners in Cuba.
It is stated once again in favor of a democratic transition and strict respect for fundamental human rights.
Let us not, for now, enter into a topic that divides us: whether the common position should be maintained or not.
Let us now focus on the issue of saving lives and human rights.
And we speak of a period of calm reflection in which we seek points of convergence on the issues of future policy toward Cuba.
However, this text also has an additional important value.
The fact that six political groups have put forward the proposal reflects a new era of broad consensus in this Chamber on the issue of human rights.

Let it never again be claimed that European democratic right-wing forces submit to the commands of Yankee imperialism, but let it also never again be said that social democrats are complacent or complicit with communist dictatorships.
Simply because both statements are false, and because the victims of the liberticides, wherever they may be, must know that we will stand united in unconditional defense of their cause.

Mr. President, finally, I would like to thank Mr. Salafranca, who has negotiated this text on behalf of the European People's Party, Ms. Weber from the Group of the Alliance of Liberals and Democrats, and Mr.

Mr. Romévez, on behalf of the Greens Group, to Mr. Kasutnik of the Conservative and Reformist Group, and to other colleagues who have participated in this difficult, complicated, yet I hope tomorrow successful effort.

Lastly, I would also like to thank the President of the Spanish Government and President of the European Union Council, Mr. José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero, for his encouragement and support in advancing this resolution which we are debating today.
History will vindicate me was the famous plea delivered by a young lawyer that stirred his people to their core.
History, in fact, exonerates it from its rebellion against tyranny and later against the American blockade.
But with the same emphatic judgment, this Parliament, which represents the largest area of freedom and democracy in the world, condemns the dictatorship.
What does the people of Cuba suffer? The violation of human rights on the island? The disregard for political prisoners? The contempt toward its exiled nationals?
The judgment of history is clear.
With this resolution, deputies from all political ideologies stand in solidarity with the Cubans who are fighting.
Every possible means must be used to avoid the brutal repression they endure, even transcending the paralyzing stance of common consensus.
I honor the poet Raúl Rivero in the final verse of his Habana. Do not impose taxes on affection, on emptiness, on asphyxiation, on bitterness.
The ruins of the homeland are safe.
Calm down, comrades.
We are leaving already.
This week, International Women's Day was celebrated.
In this Parliament, we saw many posters in all the languages of the European Union saying yes, violence against women can be ended.

Today we are discussing violence in Mexico, and it seems natural to acknowledge that Mexican women, both indigenous and non-indigenous, are the victims least protected by local and federal authorities.
Mexican women are victims of all forms of violence, ranging from domestic violence to sexual violence and torture carried out by members of the army or police, up to homicide.
Unfortunately, to date, the responses by Mexican authorities have been ineffective, which is why it is accurate to speak of near-total impunity.
Our Resolution urges the Mexican Government to combat femicides—a strong yet accurate term, given that nearly all murders committed against women remain unpunished.
An example from the State of Mexico: in the past four years, only 11 of the criminals who murdered nearly 700 women have been convicted.
At the same time, it appears that women are the preferred victims of the judicial system.
There is no other way to explain how it was possible that two indigenous women, Alberta Alcántara and Teresa González, were sentenced to twenty-one years in prison and up to
They have now spent more than three years in prison for kidnappings and robberies, during an incident at a market involving six agents from the Federal Investigation Agency.
I wish the judge, who will rule on the appeal in this case within a week, would recognize the absurdity and injustice of the situation. But violence against women begins within the family.

I hope that Mexican legislators soon improve the General Law on Women's Access to a Life Free from Violence, so that the types of violence can be clearly defined and mechanisms established to ensure coherence among the regulations.
Likewise, it would be customary to establish that domestic violence and spousal rape are criminal offenses.
Moreover, for Mexican society to change and reject violence against women, it is essential that schools make this issue a primary concern in the education of young people.
Madam President, the truth is that what I would first like to do this evening is thank Mr. Scotty for his excellent willingness to negotiate this report.
It has been a true pleasure working with him and with the other spokespersons of the parliamentary groups.

We are proud of the position that will be demonstrated tomorrow in the vote in this European Parliament, and we are proud of a stance such as this that defends quality, bets on the development and valorization of rural areas, not only as

Not as numerous witnesses to a traditional European culture, but as genuine agents generating wealth and employment—employment that we so desperately lack at this moment of economic crisis and dramatic unemployment levels in some Member States.
We also appreciate the defense upheld by this European Parliament and the position it has already taken—clearly stated by the Committee on Agriculture—of advocating for the highest possible quality.
And at that highest level, we certainly advocate maintaining the distinction between denominations of origin and protected geographical indications.
Of course we reject the idea of lowering quality standards, and we want community-produced work to receive attention, recognition, and proper valuation.
I welcome the words of Commissioner Cholo.
It seemed to me, of course, a pleasure to hear him speak about the bet on the future of European agriculture.
Of course, we hope that you will hear that voice from this Parliament, which will become clear tomorrow in that vote.

And, finally, we would like to say that we hope the European Commission never again anticipates this Parliament by banning measures such as, for example, marketing standards for fruits and vegetables, which have done so much harm to European free competition.
We want market imbalances to be corrected, and we also want to commit to quality, diversity, food security, and an appropriate level of production.
We also want community-produced goods and the quality of those community-based agricultural and food productions to be defended within the World Trade Organization.
And for that, we certainly also rely on the work of Commissioner Cholos, as our producers and European consumers cannot remain defenseless any longer.

for example, monitoring passengers' rights and also mediating or requesting a truce in the various collective conflicts in land transportation

Since I was unable to do so on March 11th, International Day of the Victims of Terrorism, I would like today, from this Parliament, to pay them a well-deserved tribute that acknowledges the immense sacrifice of those who have paid the highest price in the name of freedom.

It is urgent to begin working on a directive that protects the rights of victims of terrorism, who are always the great forgotten ones and endure the humiliation of being unjustly equated with their perpetrators.
It is high time these European institutions recognized the dignity of terrorism victims and assisted them in their right to protection and justice, which should soon be translated into law.

which will honor all those who make it possible, and that the first article, that support for victims of terrorism must recognize the right to justice, and that no government ever pay a political price.

I call upon all those who can defend the victims to never forget that true peace is the one born of justice, and that is the only peace we, who believe in it, must defend from Europe and from any corner of the world.

Mr. President, it has been necessary, Mr. President, to face the abyss and economic collapse in order for the heads of state and government to understand that the path is not one of greater nationalism, but of greater Europe.
At this address, measures adopted include the decline of the veto right in the European Council or the seed of a European public treasury.
Finally, though belatedly, we have come to realize that we are all sailing on the same boat, and that it is pointless to address individual situations, especially in an improvised manner.

But I wonder whether this attitude is merely a parenthesis or, on the contrary, the beginning of a genuine European economic governance, because what remains, dear colleagues, is governance, and what is lacking is authentic government if we are to remain faithful to the legacy of the founding fathers.

Therefore, from this Parliament we must promote all those reforms aimed at strengthening Europe and turning budgetary and fiscal discipline into reality, setting aside, of course, nostalgic notions of sovereignty.

Ladies and gentlemen, colleagues, strengthening the European Commission and strengthening this Parliament is the only correct recipe for building a Europe that, otherwise, would fade away like a fleeting shadow.

Today more than ever, the Union for the Mediterranean is necessary, along with the strengthening of major projects and institutional development.
Today more than ever, it is necessary to find solutions to exiting the crisis, to research, to climate change, to water, to new energy models, and to sustainable development.

And to this end, it is necessary to promote and finalize commercial and economic relations in the region through short- and long-term measures, such as establishing a free trade area, eliminating tariff barriers, and opening up north-south and south-south markets.
The Barcelona Summit, the Spanish Presidency, will provide the opportunity for a new commitment, a new situation, a new shared approach, and the democratization of economic and social solutions.
The Barcelona Summit could place us in a position to lead the Mediterranean.
Madam President, among the trans-European transport corridors is the railway corridor known as the Mediterranean Corridor.
It is a vital communication link that will connect North Africa with Europe.
For Spain, it is vital because this axis is a priority, as it would connect Algeciras with Europe.
Not excluding other priority routes, it would pass through Almería, Murcia, the Valencian Community, and Catalonia.
And why did Commissioner Reding say so? She mentioned greater economic activity and significant concentration of land transport.
This is one of the two conditions.
The possibility arose of communicating with various ports in the Mediterranean region.
An economic boost is needed, and competitiveness is also essential, as the commissioner herself stated, since this region accounts for more than 50 percent of Spain's exports.
Therefore, if we enhance the theme of a strategic railway axis across the Mediterranean, we will increase competitiveness for Spain and greater competitiveness for Europe.

the obligation to establish such profiles, as well as their conditions of use, by January 19, 2009, as prescribed by Regulation No 1924

And reopening this debate may prove dangerous and necessary, and could create problems for the circulation of products.
