We are indeed referring to those factors that affect cholesterol, to all those substances that influence what we have mentioned—the issue of obesity and human health.

Yes, but some other foods, along with certain additional details—such as the place of origin, when not misleading—or, for example, with meat, specifying the place of birth, the place of transit, the grazing location, and the place of slaughter of the animals, can be very informative, but ultimately may lead to confusion.

It is up to us to ensure that we are practical, and that this Regulation serves as the one that harmonizes and informs consumers, or else, in the alternative, that it further confuses them and imposes issues that, far from achieving better health accounts, will result in greater confusion.

That is why I ask, for example, that we adopt the GDA—Guideline Daily Amount—instead of traffic lights, because it is very clear that if you consume this amount or double it, you will gain weight; therefore, consume only one.
The amount of daily intake in relation to all types of codes is what we have proposed, in order not to further confuse the consumer.
Madam President, many thanks to you, Mr. Martín Fraguero, for being here today, on behalf of the Spanish Presidency, in such a report, Madam Commissioner for External Affairs.
Mr. Milana, congratulations on your ability to reach a common agreement, to bring together all interests, and to place on the table a own-initiative report—one of the first own-initiative reports.
The development of aquaculture is important.

It is necessary due to the increasing consumption of fish and the stagnation of fishing extraction capacity; however, I wish to emphasize at this moment the economic importance of this activity, which generates economic wealth and employment.
It is important to ensure sanitary safeguards, it is important to ensure sustainability, and it is important to protect the marine environment.
The image of aquaculture must be strengthened, and the scientific and research sector must also be strengthened.
Aquaculture is an opportunity, as Mr. Martin Frareiro has said within the CAP, as the Commissioner herself stated.

A coherent framework for coordination must be established, and the possibility of regularization must be provided, because aquaculture can ensure sufficiency, sustainability, and economic resources, and I believe this is important for Europe.
Mr. President, in my opinion, this excellent report outlines the guiding principles that should inspire the new White Paper on Transport, shaping European transport policy for the next ten years.

This report highlights several key points worthy of note: the necessity of a financial fund as an indispensable instrument, the challenge posed by the growing demand for freight transportation, and the pursuit of convenient solutions that
the pressing need for sufficient and safe rest areas in road transport, and the accurate diagnosis, in my view, that mere competition among the different sectors should not be allowed.

not only among the modes, but rather complement one another in seeking criteria for efficiency. The speaker's assertion, courageous and just in my view, that rail or maritime transport is not always more environmentally friendly than road transport.
In short, the concept of an efficient community is a forward-looking idea, and in my opinion, it undoubtedly must be supported with the utmost rigor.
Is yours, Mr. President, a mistake in framing?
Did you really think you would preside over Europe and influence the mechanisms that drive the complex European machinery?
He even came to believe, because those close to you allowed it to be proclaimed, that the world was going to experience a stellar moment under your presidency.
None of this has come true.
We will make a few clarifications.

During these past six months of Spanish presidency, I would like to distinguish, on one hand, between the political direction of the presidency and, on the other, the work carried out by the officials who have been operating behind the scenes.
It is the first one that has failed, and this is due to the absence of proper leadership from the Presidency of the Government, which has instead been shaped more by political swings.
However, I wish to highlight the efforts of the officials, their valuable commitment to serving Europe and Spain.
And the fact is, Mr. President, Spain has excellent professional civil servants, and if there is anything to regret, it is that in everyday reality they are too often sidelined.
That said, it is appropriate to acknowledge that some commendable initiatives have been taken during these six months.
Among them, I am pleased to cite the organ donation and transplantation plan, and the treatment afforded to patients' rights in cross-border healthcare.
Even less solid have been the agreements regarding the new 2020 Strategy, and especially those relating to the European External Action Service, both of which remain too fragile at this stage.
Regrettably, the outcome regarding the European protection order for victims of gender-based violence has been disappointing, and the same bleak result has been produced by the
A European patent, essential for the livelihood of thousands of businesses, partially blocked by linguistic issues encouraged by Spanish legislation.

But all major political actions have ended in spectacular failure. From the Mediterranean summit to the European Union summit with the United States, the latter being the one about which its own government was responsible for generating exaggerated expectations.
However, we must agree that being unable to implement their policy regarding Cuba has been highly beneficial for the Cubans.
Lastly, the progress achieved on the path toward better European economic governance cannot in any way be attributed to her presidency, conspicuously absent on the most relevant issues.
Goodbye, then, to the Spanish presidency.

A positive conclusion I do wish to draw from this experience is my fantasy as a committed Europeanist, which is excited by the thought that it might signal the end of rotating presidencies as they have been conceived until now.
This would be good news, for as long as Europe fails to strengthen its common institutions, solidly uniting its fragments, it will continue to risk being engulfed by the voids of irrelevance.
Mr. President, believe me, Mr. Zapatero, I would have preferred to deliver a different speech today.
Your Lordship proposed a semester filled with ambition and set such a high standard in one's aspirations that, when compared to what was actually achieved, disappointment is inevitable.
The truth is that Your Lordship has been unable to overcome a complex situation, one that required leadership and credibility.
Your Lordship has at times recalled that the European Union is a club with rules, and I agree.
But it was to his lordship that it fell to enforce compliance with those rules.
The reality is that it was to the Government of Spain that they urgently demanded the fulfillment of its obligations.
In this painting, positive achievements that have not been lacking have been overshadowed.
Your Lordship has had time to recount them; I have not.
Democracy, Your Lordships, is a system governed by public opinion.

And this is what appears to be Obama's disdain, the cancellation of the Mediterranean Summit, and the announced European Union's distancing from the United States constitute failures of great significance.

The rushed attempts to close deals at any price have not enhanced the image of Spanish management, which has been labeled "Chapucero"—a term denoting a makeshift approach to achieving any respectable goal.
Spain is a great nation and will remain so, despite poor management during this period.
Our diplomats and officials have maintained their standards, and thanks to this, Spain's prestige will endure.
The truth is that the evaluation of this presidency will weigh more on Spain's situation than on what was or was not accomplished here.
It would be difficult for someone who has placed their country in a state of uncertainty and unease to effectively lead such a challenging enterprise.
At best, this presidency has sadly proven irrelevant.
Mr. Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen, in the coming months we will fully engage in the debate on the new CAP.

It is important to reach a good agreement, so I hope the Belgian Presidency will achieve what the Spanish Presidency did not, namely, steer the discussions in such a way that Member States commit to maintaining, in the future, a coherent policy

A truly robust common agriculture, with sufficient funding to meet the needs of all twenty-seven Member States, both new and old, so that we do not repeat what happened in 2005, when funds were insufficient and we had to invent modulation.

In the upcoming reform of the CAP, we are wagering the viability of a sector that not only provides intangible goods and services, such as environmental stewardship, but should also ensure the supply of food to European citizens.

We have already witnessed painful situations of scarcity in the past, which is why it is necessary for the European Parliament to ensure security and supply based on community production as the primary objective of the upcoming reform, without neglecting other contributions such as the fight against climate change.

Madam President, Commissioner, colleagues, I agree with Commissioner Cholos that this report is necessary—very necessary—and comes at an opportune moment, as the abuses committed by large-scale distribution have been demonstrated, severely disrupting the various links in the food chain.

The Bové report, which at times may go too far in attempting to be overly interventionist, must be acknowledged from the standpoint of the social market economy—such as the one advocated by the European People's Party—for its inclusion of responses to the legitimate demands of European producers.

Moreover, it brings transparency to the various parts of the food chain, which is always beneficial, and provides significantly greater opportunities and support for the weaker sectors within that chain, while also clearly supporting European consumers in gaining better knowledge of the chain.

Madam President, the European food industry could have been given greater consideration in this report—perhaps it would have been very beneficial—but this report also includes measures to curb price volatility, which was greatly necessary.

Measures are also being taken to ensure equity in those links of the chain, as called for by the Environmental Commission, and there are also demands to combat abusive and unfair practices by large retailers.
It is high time to establish a list of abusive practices and to prohibit them unequivocally through European legislation.
It is also important to implement measures to prevent difficult practices through white-labeling, which go against competition and violate intellectual property law.

For all these reasons, we must support the Bové report and all the colleagues from the different parliamentary committees—INCO, ENVI, or Agriculture—who have worked diligently to ensure this report finally sees the light of day and helps European producers move forward.
The alarm bell sounds, and it is true
And frustration is also evident, as heard in the speeches delivered during this plenary session, regarding the failure to achieve the Millennium Development Goals.
And the truth is that what we are risking is great.

Our natural environment is so fragile that, if one day the big fish decided it would no longer consume the small, we would have to pay for it all out of our own pockets, using our health and well-being as the currency.
The European Union is today a leader in the fight against climate change and has been for some time a pioneer in conservation measures.

Yet, as demonstrated by the unmet objectives, it appears that willpower was lacking—not only in implementation and coordination, which have also been mentioned here, but also in expanding beyond our extremely limited conservation zones.
Certainly, biodiversity is not a museum piece that can be confined to a ghettoized area, as sometimes appears to happen, merely glancing at it now and then.
Biodiversity thrives and knows no borders; it spans the entire Earth in a domino effect, such that if one piece falls, all the others fall as well.

I draw attention to the need for a more global and communal will, particularly with regard to land, because subsidiarity, as the sole method for managing land or forests, could become one of the new formidable enemies of biodiversity.

That is why I draw your attention to paragraph 67 and the possible vote that may follow, and I ask you to reject the second part, as I am convinced that a common policy on land would contribute to the conservation of the entire territory.

A European world so varied, so diverse for better and for worse, and so lacking in political will on the part of the states, yet so in need of ever greater European Union, with all the implications of the term, including greater Union also for the land.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, first of all, I would like to highlight the work of the rapporteur, Mrs. McAvan, not only in substance but also in form, as she has carried out her task in collaboration with all the shadow rapporteurs.
I would also like to thank the experts from the Spanish administration for their contribution to this agreement.

The objective of this European Union pharmacovigilance system is to strengthen the monitoring, transparency, and communication of the safety of food and medicinal products after they have been marketed, particularly with regard to their risk profiles and any adverse reactions they may cause.

It is important to highlight the establishment of the European Pharmacovigilance Committee, which will assess and issue recommendations to the European Medicines Agency regarding safety issues that may arise with medicines marketed in the European Union.

It is very important that the solution reached regarding the reporting of suspected adverse reactions to the pharmacovigilance system, both by the Member States and by the marketing authorization holders, has been established.

Ultravigilance will be the sole source of information, but at the same time, the competent authority of the Member State where the adverse reaction is suspected will be notified, and this is very important.
It is also worth highlighting the opportunity afforded to consumers to report any adverse reaction, and in this regard, the creation and management by the Agency of a portal
and European portal on medicine safety, which in turn links to the portals of each Member State, will help improve citizens' understanding of the safety of the medicines they consume.
And finally, we cannot overlook the fact that, since pharmacovigilance plays a crucial role in protecting public health, the adoption of the Directive and the
This Directive and this Regulation will significantly contribute to this goal by improving not only current procedures but also the coordination of actions among the various Member States.

Mr. President, I highly appreciate the rapporteur's effort in accepting certain suggestions, and naturally, her defense of copyright, which I share; however, the inclusion of the infringing and sanctionable nature of certain exchanges, and the

The recognition of sanctioning authority granted to suppliers, as well as the exclusion of mass exchanges as a potential violation in the alternative resolution, makes it impossible to vote in favor of either of the two reports in Liza.
The Gallo report has generated considerable interest and might have been timely, but the heart of the debate is too deeply entrenched in the argument for or against the French law known as

Pi, from the perspective of internet connection cutoff for offenders, without there having yet been a genuine European debate that takes into account the reality of other advancements in national legislation.

I hope we achieve a common position within the Socialist Group and a majority in the European Parliament during the debates and amendments on the Commission's proposed directive, which will soon reach the Parliament, as announced by Commissioner Barnier.

On this occasion, we will strive to achieve a more balanced position than the one we have today, one that takes into account both the rights of Internet users and the essential respect for intellectual property.
Mr. President, I congratulate those who have worked on this report, on this financial package, particularly the officials and Members of this Parliament.
It has taken a financial crisis of enormous proportions for Europe to wake up.
However, an opportunity has been lost, in part, to strengthen Europe's common institutions, because, on one hand, these new supervisory authorities have a limited scope of competence

limited compared to similar authorities of Member States, and it is regrettable as well that progress has not been made toward an integration similar to that achieved with the system of central banks.
We have made progress, but it is essential to continue climbing the mountain.

Madam President, as a member of the Human Rights Committee, as a Spanish and European citizen, and as a victim of terrorism since the terrorist organization ETA murdered my brother and his wife in Seville, I address this Parliament to denounce the Government of Venezuela and its president for having allowed terrorists to

The ballots were trained in their Venezuelan homeland under the command of the alleged terrorist Cubilla, who works for Hugo Chávez's government, despite an international arrest warrant outstanding against him, constituting a serious violation of the fundamental right to security of any European citizen.
I demand that the Government of Spain take decisive action in response to this serious humiliation of all Spaniards.
Zapatero cannot remain silent while the Venezuelan ambassador labels our civil guards as torturers, men who have been and will continue to be a model of bravery in the fight against ETA.
It cannot turn a blind eye to this growing cooperation between ETA and the FARC on Venezuelan soil, as Judge Eloy Velasco has already denounced.
Zapatero cannot shake hands with a leader like Chávez, who is turning Venezuela into a refuge for terrorists.

The president of a country like Spain, where terrorism has caused so much suffering, cannot continue to ignore this issue and should provide a response worthy of the situation—one that any ordinary citizen would give if given the chance.
Mr. President, on February 14, 2008, as a Valentine's Day gift, the Conference of Presidents decided that intergroups would meet only on Thursdays.
Since then, we have attempted to resolve this issue.
We have not achieved it.
Today, Mr. President, the committees have to meet on a date that is not appropriate.
They almost never have rooms and never have interpretation.

Today I speak not only as President of the Intergroup on Poverty and Human Rights, but also with the mandate of the majority of the Interparliamentary Groups in this Chamber, and therefore on behalf of the Members of this Chamber who are part of those Interparliamentary Groups.
And I solemnly request that the Conference of Presidents reconsider and revoke the Decision of 14 February 2008 and restore the previous situation of freedom for the intergroups.
And if it does not, I gently say that we will bring the matter before the full assembly of this House, and it will decide who in this House is sovereign—that is, this Parliament.
Mr. President, the International Day against the Trafficking and Exploitation of Persons has highlighted data that must compel us to act decisively.
Over 90 percent of the prostitution carried out in Europe stems from blackmail and extortion.

And, given these facts, we should ask ourselves whether we are doing everything necessary to protect the dignity and integrity of thousands of women whose rights are also safeguarded by the Fundamental Charter adopted through the Treaty of Lisbon.
Because we are not talking about the world's oldest profession, but rather the only form of slavery that we have failed to eradicate from old Europe.
Countries such as Spain are making significant efforts in this regard by pursuing traffickers, raising public awareness, and promoting reintegration programs for female victims.
But it is not enough.
Europe must become a single space of action and commitment.

The upcoming Directive on Treatment presents an opportunity to address demand through pedagogy and to make it more difficult to access, for example, the availability of advertising across all types of media, while also enhancing bilateral cooperation with third countries to combat this scourge at its source.
We have an opportunity
It draws the attention of this Parliament to continue being at the forefront of citizens' rights.
Madam President, we are all in agreement that the external service represents a historic opportunity for European integration.

But to achieve this, it is essential to act with broad vision, as now is not the time for internal struggles over resources that are temporarily transferred or retained, nor for conflicts among the various European institutions.
What is required now, by contrast, is to have a comprehensive overview of what the European Union should be in the world, that is, to do precisely the opposite of
And this is precisely what occurred a few days ago, on the occasion of the awarding of the Nobel Peace Prize, when far too many voices were heard from Europe, and not all of them courageous.
Noteworthy is one exception: the President of this Parliament, Mr. Buzek, is the author of a brilliant and worthy statement.

Mr. President, the Regulation we are debating today is of vital importance for the future of an industry—cutlery and related sectors—that in my region, the land of Don Quixote, specifically in Albacete, represents not only the survival of more than eight thousand jobs but also an element of identity and a forward-looking commitment to craftsmanship as a source of employment.
The Meidín knife certification has long been claimed by social groups such as Precu, and contested by the Government of Castilla-La Mancha with the unanimous support of its regional Parliament.
A border seal that is not interventionist is a commitment to transparency in commercial rules and the fight against unfair competition.

It involves enhancing consumers' decision-making capacity when choosing a product by encouraging them to consider not only the final price, but also the quality brand of the place of origin or the social conditions under which the product is made.

And above all, it will represent a new horizon of optimism for many people who are going through difficult times, yet today look to us thinking that we have not remained indifferent in our commitment to a fairer trade—yes, but also in our effort to add value to those who, in addition to producing, do so with respect for social rights and under the best working conditions for European workers.
That is why I thank the rapporteur and Mr. Menéndez del Valle for their political support and social awareness on this important issue for my homeland.
Mr. President, let me see if I can bring a bit of calm to this lively debate.
It is regrettable that some countries and some states comprising it continue to be present at this G20 forum alongside the European Union.
Despite this anomaly, which certainly undermines Europe's image in the world, it would be beneficial if at least a common global position existed within that international forum.
And, in my opinion, what would that common position be?

In my modest opinion, I believe that first, the adoption of a global agreement to address the consequences of the crisis cannot be delayed, and this must go beyond merely a financial agreement. Second, Europe must maintain the euro as a reference currency, or, if preferred, as an anchor for

To prevent market turbulence from dragging us down and causing us to repeat the mistakes made throughout the twentieth century, the euro, thirdly, must serve as a herald of what Europe should represent in the world of democratic values and freedoms.
public.
In summary, it must be understood that in the global world, in this global game, those who play only national cards lose.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen, I believe we are in a position to congratulate the rapporteur, Mrs. Meissner.
I believe different positions were identified, and she has reached compromise solutions that allow us all to come together on an important matter.
Ladies and gentlemen, the Transport Committee has decided that all seas should be similarly protected, and for this purpose invokes that otherwise an unfair competition situation would arise.
We accept the majority's position in favor of a legal situation with uniform rules for the entire Union, but I shall not hesitate to clearly point out that it also entails a certain lack of fairness.
It is evident that the Baltic Sea, the North Sea, and the English Channel are more polluted areas, and monitoring of sulfur limit values must be stricter in these cases.
Positive value should be placed on measures relating to ports, as they impact solutions for reducing transportation times and handling costs.
In any case, I will not tire of reiterating that at this point we have an outstanding task.
Without a determined liberalization of the ports, they will not be able to expand the central role they must play within the context of convenience.
Welcome as well, Your Lordships, to the drive behind a strategy aimed at modifying the specific impacts of climate change.
I believe this is a positive report, which should be approved.
But we cannot forget that it occurs in the context of a serious disaster, namely, that of the Gulf of Mexico.

And in this regard, the Commission is urged to assess whether the framework for the review of the European Maritime Safety Agency's Regulation strengthens the Agency's role in preventive inspections and subsequent oil spill cleanup tasks.
In my personal opinion, Madam Commissioner, yes, and those tasks should be reinforced with the mandate necessary for that purpose.

Mr. President, integrated maritime policy is a cross-cutting, regional, and sectoral policy, which must include fisheries policy and aquaculture, and this should not be subordinated to other policies.
We all must address environmental and economic challenges, both for society at large and for the fishing sector. New technologies, fishing gear, species development, a marine economy plan...

to weave greater and better employment, quality supplies, international cooperation that prevents piracy, and to promote governance of our seas, which know no boundaries and must not face sustainability challenges, as in the case of Iceland.
This would mean working toward an integrated maritime policy; this would mean working for the environment and sustainability.
Madam President, recently the Church of Our Lady of Sorrows in Baghdad was attacked, and 58 people, mostly women and children, were murdered solely because they were Christians.
Al Qaeda has proudly claimed responsibility for the feat.
It is not an isolated incident, but the culmination of persecutions and attacks against Christians in countries with a Muslim majority.
Expulsions in Morocco, threats in Turkey, attacks in India or Nigeria.
In January, six Christians were massacred at the entrance of a church in Cairo.
Cairo: How long will the complicit silence of Western democracies continue?
Fortunately, President Buzek has made an institutional statement.
The condemnation statement by High Representative Ms. Ashton has been limited to a condemnation of a terrorist act.
Why is the mobile phone not cited? The condition of being Christians, which is the unequivocal cause of the massacre.
I have not heard anything from Mr. Zapatero, I have not heard anything from President Erdogan.
And, Erdogan, what is the purpose of the Alliance of Civilizations?
I feel shame for our foolishness and our cowardice.

Inevitably, Oriana Fallaci comes to mind—the wept-over and misunderstood journalist who foretold the arrival of Eurabia and was nearly stoned for having the courage to expose what is happening in complex Europe.
Also to the Lady Commissioner. I share the wish of this Chamber.
I want the Europeans' proposals to receive the support they deserve in Cancún.
Regarding this debate, it is essential to emphasize the importance of cities in preserving the environment.

There is a pact among more than a thousand mayors from the European Union, demonstrating that these authorities acknowledge the existence of the problem, and their actions will be decisive if we are to succeed.
All the grand words we use are of no value if we do not have the cooperation of the mayors of the cities.
To this end, it is very important that emissions be widely disseminated, as cities produce nearly 80 percent of greenhouse gases.
Today, there is no such dissemination, nor, worse still, a common standard for measuring these emissions themselves.
There is a nonprofit organization called the CO2 Outreach Project, which already hosts information from thousands of companies and, in my opinion, could also be used by municipalities.
Without their cooperation, without the cooperation of these municipalities and mayors, environmental action amounts to smoke—the very smoke we seek to escape.

Mr. President, some have argued that the condemnation of Morocco is too lenient given the scale of human rights repression in the Sahara, where Morocco is nothing more than an occupying country without sovereignty or any legal title whatsoever.
But what matters most is the message that the entire Europe and this Parliament can no longer tolerate your violence.
Yesterday I was speaking about the Hague Tribunal in this very Parliament.

The international community, which sadly failed to prevent the genocide in Rwanda, Yugoslavia, and Darfur, I hope will avoid another such tragedy in Western Sahara, and that we will not have to see it again before another tribunal, also in The Hague, as I said, but this time for war crimes.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, the Directive adopted in 2009 was a success because it updates the regulatory framework governing toy safety.
It is a significant step in the right direction, as it indicates the path to follow, but it is not enough.
We must always remain vigilant and attentive to ensure that toys marketed do not endanger children's safety and health.
All toys available on the market must comply with current legislation.
We must review and improve surveillance mechanisms to detect hazardous toys, whether due to substances considered toxic, fragrances, or other dangerous substances such as HPA compounds.
It is necessary that the guidelines to be followed be clearer regarding the minimum age warning and the dangers arising from improper use.
It is necessary to ensure that the recommended age for toys is based on studies of children's behavior.
We must be able to adapt our regulations to scientific advancements supported by robust studies, and become more rigorous in monitoring the market and the importation of toys.
It is necessary to work together and strengthen cooperation among Member States, as well as ensure joint efforts by customs authorities to monitor the supply chain of imported toys.
Citizens are preparing to make their holiday purchases, and we must assure them that the products they buy meet all safety requirements.
In this debate, it has become clear that we all agree on supporting the objective of this report.
Different political groups have shown their support for this Directive, which provides greater protection for victims.
Therefore, I wonder what obstacle exists in approving this instrument.
I repeat: What fears are there that something as necessary and straightforward as ensuring equal protection for victims throughout Europe will ultimately become a reality?
Having hoped that this Parliament will overwhelmingly approve this report, I turn to the Council and the Commission, reminding them that we have
We have surpassed the legal constraints in our negotiations, and I applaud the Commission for acknowledging our efforts, just as we recognize the work it will undertake in the future.

We therefore need not exclude ourselves, but rather support one another, approving this more concrete instrument today and tomorrow the broader package of measures on victim assistance that the Commission will present, to which I commit myself to contributing.
Therefore, let us be courageous and responsible today, and help all those who have the right to live in a safer Europe.
Let us help everyone, I repeat, whether men, women, or children.
Let us help those who today look to us, so that we may give them a better life, a life that restores their freedom, their security, and above all, their dignity.

Madam President, the Group of the European People's Party will enthusiastically support this report, and my congratulations first of all to the rapporteurs, the Commission, and the Council for this agreement.
We coined, Your Lordships, the term "democratic deficit" in the 1980s.
With it, we wanted to convey that in the European Union, decisions were made solely by the Council of Ministers upon proposals from the Commission, and this Parliament was merely consulted.
The struggle of this Parliament since the 1980s, through the Maastricht Treaty, the Treaty of Amsterdam, the Treaty of Nice, the Constitutional Treaty, and the Treaty of Lisbon, has been
bridge that democratic deficit. And I believe that with the Treaty of Lisbon, with the European Parliament's new powers, and with the involvement of national parliaments, we have achieved that goal.

And we conclude this today with this citizen legislative initiative, which, in the work of the Convention, was made possible by the excellent cooperation between national parliaments and the European Parliament.

I would like to recall here the names of Jürgen Meyer, our German Social Democrat colleague, and Annan Lamassoure, who were the ones who brought forward that initiative at the penultimate Agreement and ensured its approval.
Therefore, I believe we have completed the circle, Madam President.
I believe that today, when we vote on this initiative, we will have delivered, placed on the table, the death certificate of the concept of democratic decision-making.
It is now our turn, Your Lordships, to act responsibly, and today we also have the opportunity to do so.
By voting on the Union's budget, we will fulfill what is expected of us as a responsible Parliament and a co-decision-making Parliament.
I believe it is a good day for Europe, Ladies and Gentlemen.
Mr. President, in January 2012, up to 400 Spanish egg producers—approximately 30 percent of the total in my country—could disappear, resulting in the loss of 300 tons of egg production.

And the European Union could cease producing those 80 million eggs, those 2 million tons, and we would merely end up having them covered by imports from third countries, whose animal welfare standards are significantly lower than those of the European Union, if we do not act with great intelligence.
This 1999 Directive, which requires increasing the space in cages for laying hens, obliges us to act with intelligence and a pragmatic vision, because otherwise...

We will not only weaken European production and create additional opportunities, commercial opportunities, for third countries, where bird welfare standards are infinitely worse than the current ones we have in the European Union.

It is a directive that requires enormous efforts from European producers. In Spain alone, we can estimate the cost to rise to approximately 600 million euros, but we must also remember that it has an economic impact extending to the egg-product industry and the food industry as a whole.

I therefore request the support of this House for the Popular Group's amendment to paragraph 2, which calls for seeking a solution, at the very least, for responsible farming operations run by responsible operators who are undergoing transformation and will be nearly completed by January 2012.

We must support them in transforming those facilities and allow them sufficient time to complete the process, thus avoiding irreparable harm to farms and preventing an abrupt shortage in the community market, which would consequently lead to higher prices for consumers.
It is about respecting the Directive, giving an opportunity to the egg and egg product industry, while also upholding animal welfare rights and consumers' right to reasonable prices.

Mr. President, I believe it is only fair to thank the rapporteurs, particularly Mrs. Grossetête and Mrs. Dagmar Romberg from my own Group, for the work they have done to move this legislation out of stagnation and make it effective, as their efforts have enabled the inclusion of the right to public health as a legal basis.

And to establish a new direction that enables us to lay the foundations ensuring citizens the security and quality they choose at any given time, particularly because it also allows for clear regulation of the roles of States—both those of origin, which are responsible for making payments, and those receiving treatment.

But I believe we should not be satisfied merely with this; rather, this must mark the beginning of a much greater advancement that, as Mrs. Rompelin stated, transforms this directive into a mere formality, since the level of equality achieved in all Member States makes it unnecessary for each citizen to travel to every country.

And in this struggle, we must achieve a standard that ensures greater protection of citizens' rights regarding the benefits they are entitled to receive, including sensitivity and security, rather than merely the existing right to mobility between Member States.
Equality is what will make Union possible, and if not, we will be taking very small steps.

Madam President, the Spanish Socialist delegation has voted in favor, as has the rest of our group, but I would not leave here without expressing my concern that these provisions have been included in this Directive
the issue of organ transplants, contrary to the position held by the Committee on the Environment, resulting from the Council's persistent insistence on including transplants in this Directive.

I hope this inclusion proves to be as highly effective as the good legislation we adopted in this very same plenary session, both the Transplant Directive and the Action Plan, just a short time ago and with near-universal support.

And I emphasize that we will remain vigilant to ensure that this inclusion does not undermine the excellent work carried out by organizations such as Spain's National Transplant Organization, and certainly not the goal of saving twenty thousand lives that we had both in the Transplant Directive and in the Action Plan, which I repeat was approved here.
Therefore, I make this clear and state that we will remain attentive to the implementation of this Directive in this regard.
Mr. President, today, moreover, is also European Day against Sexual Health.
Ladies and gentlemen, the data are alarming. The World Health Organization estimates that over 300 million people inadvertently contract a sexually transmitted disease each year.

In Europe, as the European Forum on Sexual Health warns, it is concerning that the number of unwanted pregnancies among adolescents is increasing due to an excessive lack of education and information among our young people.

Indeed, as the Commission points out, there are various and diverse inequalities across Europe in access to sexual health services, depending on the country and, within each country, according to citizens' income levels.
To combat all of this, a European strategy is required.
Only in five member states are such plans in place, but in all of them, the number of unwanted pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, and abortions has been reduced.
A common strategy that emphasizes prevention through the educational sector, expands information policies, increases efforts in family planning, and improves access to contraceptive methods.
A comprehensive and ambitious strategy, free from taboos, to ensure every citizen's rights to sexual health under the safest and most hygienic conditions.

Mr. President, at a moment of intense social tensions, one cannot but enthusiastically welcome the major social consensus agreement in Spain between the Government, trade unions, and employers' associations.
This agreement is exemplary for Europe, where for some time now no such milestones have been achieved.

Also worth noting are the arguments put forward by Cándido Méndez, one of the main participants in that Spanish Pact, who attended the Economic and Social Council meeting in Brussels on Tuesday, February 8.
Mr. Candido then emphasized the need to extend cooperation to a European scale and the importance of having the voice and initiatives of trade unions in our Europe.

Mr. President, setting aside the differences regarding terrorism and immigration, I have observed that the European Union unfortunately moves forward only in response to emergency situations, such as a serious aviation accident.

an attack or a massive landing of immigrants, such as the one currently taking place in Lampedusa, where the exodus of Tunisian citizens toward the Italian coast could lead to a chronicle of a foretold death.

The journey of hope may end in a cursed journey, and it will weigh upon our conscience, upon that of everyone who has witnessed these ongoing, uncontrolled flows and has not said enough "enough" with sufficient authority.
When it happens, we will be shocked, but the next day we will forget.

European citizens have both the obligation and the right to know that the Mediterranean strategy is not working, and that common migration policy will remain a dream as long as European countries fail to commit sufficient financial resources to make Frontex effective.
Speaking costs nothing, but action is required.
Do you truly believe that Frontex can currently guarantee the security of Europe's borders?
I don't think so.

Let us hope that the citizens of Tunisia, Egypt, and other Maghreb countries do not collectively decide to seek a better future in Europe, but rather do so in their own countries, as that is what they have fought for, and we must contribute to helping them achieve it.
Therefore, solidarity, responsibility, but above all, truth, when discussing a common European migration policy today more necessary than ever.

Madam President, it has already been stated that the backbone of pension systems in Europe is and must remain the public system, because it is the only system that ensures cohesion and, furthermore, reduces the risk of poverty in our society.

It remains noteworthy that in OECD countries, the highest levels of income equity among citizens are found in pension matters, and that due to the crisis, private pensions lost 20% of their value in 2008 alone, and still today remain far from the solvency levels required.

All of this demonstrates that, at a time of unavoidable reforms, the sustainability of our pension system must be addressed in a cross-cutting manner, taking into account, among other factors, ambitious policies on fertility or work-life balance.

also ambitious policies regarding access to and retention in employment, or even immigration policies based on integration, which would likewise ensure, from a demographic standpoint, the sustainability of our pension systems.
Lastly, I regret that the text does not place limits on the misuse of scholarship figures within companies, as these are generally excluded from all forms of social contribution.

In my view, this is essential for an entire new generation of workers who, if being required to do more in order to secure a decent pension, should likewise be required to do less in order to access decent work.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, we are at a pivotal moment in shaping the future cohesion policy, which, following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, addresses not only economic and social aspects but also territorial dimensions.
Territorial cohesion aims at achieving harmonious development among territories, enabling their citizens to fully benefit from their respective territorial particularities.
However, to achieve this goal, it is also necessary to advance cooperation among the regions of the Union that face the same challenges and issues.

This is the objective pursued by the European Union's Strategy for the Atlantic region: a shared perspective to address common challenges faced by the countries in the region, including marine research, maritime surveillance, and environmental and economic challenges.

To achieve our objectives, it is necessary to adopt an integrated strategic approach aligned with the 2020 Strategy and the European Union's policies for the period following 2013, particularly regional and maritime policies.
Mr. President, first and foremost, it is essential that this strategy be based on renewed multi-level governance, grounded in closer involvement of public authorities.
regional and local authorities, Member States, the European Union, and stakeholders from the private sector and civil society organizations, including interregional organizations and networks.
It is a very significant day to discuss equality between men and women, as we celebrate Women's Day, honoring those who a century ago fought for labor and economic rights and for women's suffrage.
We have made great progress over these hundred years, but much remains to be done, and true equality is still a dream, while the discrimination against women is a real fact.

I believe each of us, men and women alike, can do much more to achieve true emancipation for women, because there are many women around the world whose right to live is threatened, whose dignity is violated, and whose freedom is denied.
Let me take this opportunity on this day of women to express my support and hope for all the women in the North African Revolution who are fighting for their democratic rights.
Because a woman cannot be worth half a man, neither in Arab countries nor anywhere in the world.
Therefore, we are worth the same and deserve the same.
And I will continue fighting to achieve it, and as long as that equality does not exist, the Women's Day will continue to exist.
Although many may not wish to see it, it is a reality that there are women who earn half as much as men, and that domestic violence primarily affects women and children.
And I repeat, we must continue denouncing all those incidents in this Parliament, and certainly, I pledge that I will continue working.
Mr. President, I wish to express my agreement with the views of the Members who have spoken here this morning and who emphasize respect for Parliament and the Commission.
Therefore, my modest affirmative vote will depend on whether the European Council is willing to acknowledge the role of the institutions mentioned.
Because the new mechanism we are discussing must be an important component of economic governance, led by the European Union.
able to ensure coherence between the common economic policy, which must establish clear, precise, and limited objectives, and this stability instrument.

That is, we must absolutely avoid allowing this effort in which we are engaged to end up in an intergovernmental process, which is a dead end, or in a process dominated by just a few states, as this would undermine the reform.
Dear colleagues, Europe is built stone by stone, and this is one of those stones that strengthen and balance this delicate structure.
Essential points remain to be improved, so we must send the report back to the Commission.
The interparliamentary aspect of this report does not end here, then.
The initial proposal was poor.
This is what consumer associations at both the national and European levels told us.
And, in particular, my esteem for the work of the Union of Consumers of Asturias and its president, the indefatigable Dace Alonso.
We have managed to have the general principle of maximum harmonization of the former Article 4 rejected.

It must be clearly stated that maximum harmonization, by establishing identical protection levels across all Member States, would result in a reduction of protection for many consumers, as several countries have a stronger tradition of safeguarding consumer rights.

I would like to emphasize, however, that it is essential to ensure that the list of unfair contract terms is not exhaustive, allowing each Member State to increase the number of clauses it considers unacceptable and invalid in contracts with consumers.

Shift the burden of proof onto the individual clauses, so that it is the merchants who must demonstrate that clauses deviating from the generally established norms were individually negotiated, and that contractual terms must be expressed in a clear and comprehensible manner.
We remain opposed to the establishment of maximum harmonization regarding unfair terms.
Eurobarometer indicates that 79% of traders believe full harmonization in this area will have little or no effect on their activities abroad.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, allow me first of all to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr. Schwab, and Mrs. Gerhardt, on the excellent work they have accomplished.
and a general reflection.
It is not that protectionism is increasing, but rather that we must avoid cutting already-existing rights in some of the member states.
Because if we wish to establish and pursue a genuine internal market for business-to-consumer relationships, we must
simultaneously establish a balance between the high level of protection of consumer rights and the promotion of business competitiveness.
This balance entails agreeing on a minimum level of harmonization and allowing Member States the possibility of maintaining or adopting additional regulations that enhance consumer protection.
We cannot allow the adoption of a community standard to result in the lack of protection for many consumers and users.
I welcome the improvement in negotiations within the European Parliament, but I would like to emphasize that existing rights already established must not be reduced.

Mr. President, many experts are telling us that the next crisis could be an energy crisis, and furthermore, either we are not doing the necessary work to prevent it, or we are not doing it quickly enough to avoid it.
This coming Saturday, the World Forum for Nature calls on all of us to make a small gesture in the pursuit of sustainability.
Many citizens will turn off the lights for one hour, and many public administrations will follow suit.
But it is not enough.
Europe cannot continue relying on energies that are not its own, located far away, or that are unsafe.
We must help states build a new energy model in which renewable energies are the heart of the new
Moreover, we must promote awareness of new consumption guidelines, because undoubtedly, renewables and efficiency are the keys to future sustainability.

Finally, I would like to appeal to all countries of the Union to join this call, this commitment of five of them, including Spain, to increase CO2 emission reductions to 30 percent, thereby demonstrating that Europe, in addition to being a leader in freedom and rights, is also a leader in its commitment to the planet's sustainability.
Madam President, most of the times I have spoken in this Parliament, I have done so to denounce violence against women, and once again, I am here to combat it.

I must say that not only do I not grow weary of it, but I consider it essential to continue speaking publicly about this violation of human rights, as no cause can be effectively defended without a platform, and this Parliament is the best one.

It is my platform to advocate for all those women who suffer abuse daily and who need us, legislators, to compel states to punish offenders according to the severity of their crimes, as well as to assist the victims.

When it is estimated that one in four European women have experienced gender-based violence at least once in their lifetime, and that children have been affected to an equal extent, I believe it is impossible not to demand a greater commitment, as acknowledged by the Commission in its action plan.

I believe it is necessary to continue insisting, again and again, on placing victims at the center, as we already did in the European Protection Order for Victims, of which I was the rapporteur, and which I hope will become a reality as soon as possible.
This report has enjoyed broad consensus and support from the outset, demonstrating that when it comes to addressing domestic violence, which affects all members of the
family, children, elderly individuals, women, and men—should not be a subject of political opportunism, as advancing the solution to this social scourge honors us all.
The first step is to stop remaining silent, and the second is to get to work, since despite having discussed gender-based violence for decades, we have failed to reduce the number of deaths.

We must be doing something wrong, and therefore it is time to abandon demagogy and move from words to action, providing the additional support and necessary measures so that women can live with dignity and security.
And certainly, may all the victims know that I will always be here, continuing to lend them my voice until, together, we succeed in defeating this...
A terrible enemy that sadly ends the lives of so many innocents. I say enough, enough already, and I ask all of you to say it with me.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, I agree with you that we must listen to citizens and restore trust in the European project.
That is why today could be a great day for Europe and the construction of European social integration.

Because the approval of these reports not only advances our progress toward revitalizing the single market—the key element in confronting the current economic crisis—but also responds to citizens' demands for further European integration and the strengthening of social rights.
Europe must respond to new global challenges and ensure that the European economy becomes a highly competitive social market economy that generates meaningful growth with more and better employment.

However, this objective must be based on incorporating a social clause into all legislation related to the internal market, placing citizens at the center of our attention and priority and strengthening their social rights.
Finally, I would like to request that the Commission submit these proposals as soon as possible.
Mr. President, the blue tongue disease has been a constant threat to our livestock and, with it, also to our public health.

A disease against which we were fighting through the use of an activated vaccine, whose application requires the establishment of restricted zones for animal movement, as the use of these vaccines may cause unwanted virus circulation in uninfected animals.

For these reasons, the use of vaccination was restricted to those areas where infected animals had previously been found, creating a double disadvantage for livestock farmers: on one hand, the disease itself, and on the other, the associated restrictions.

However, all these problems appear to be avoidable through the new inactivated vaccine, which not only prevents disruptions that could arise in the domestic livestock market but also enables the development of new, and truly importantly, broader prevention strategies in the fight against this disease.
For all these reasons, it is of vital importance for this sector that we amend the Directive to allow its use, and that we do so as soon as possible.

Mr. President, the fulfillment of what our Treaties provide, particularly regarding the correspondence tables, cannot serve as an excuse for this regulation not entering into force before summer, because with our delay, with your delay, we would only be harming the livestock farmers.

Mr. President, first of all, I would like to thank the Committee on Petitions for the work it has carried out following the complaint submitted by FEEPEX, the Spanish Organization of Fruit and Vegetable Producers and Exporters, based on the report published in 2007 by OLAF, the European Union’s anti-fraud agency.

I believe the conclusions of that report are extremely clear and fully justify the fact that the European Commission should have already taken action long ago to clarify the functioning of the entry pricing system, at least in the tomato sector.

However, the Commission has not only failed to intervene to address potential loopholes created by the current entry price regulation, but has also negotiated a new agricultural agreement with Morocco without first resolving the shortcomings of the existing import regime.

Madam President, it is high time that the European Commission took its responsibilities seriously and began investigating where frauds occur, and eventually demanded the recovery of unpaid customs duties.
I believe that no new agreement with Morocco should be ratified under any circumstances without the issues we have outlined having been resolved.

Our objective is not to halt the signing of international agreements, but rather to ensure that such agreements do not seriously harm the interests of European productions and that the rules established in these agreements are respected.

Regarding the new agreement with Morocco, the European fruit and vegetables sector is asking only for guarantees that the agreed measures will be effectively implemented, and this is only possible through the reform of the current entry price regime.
Until we have a new entry pricing system, we cannot have a new agreement with Morocco.
We will not be able to ratify that agreement which the Commission has already finalized.

Moreover, if it is the responsibility of the Member States to carry out this control, then Member States would be required to make a significant financial effort; however, it is also true that we have been demanding for a long time that an European customs border system be established, similar to the one the United States has, in order to ensure effective control.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, addressing this report in Parliament has been complex, primarily because these measures require the approval of the World Trade Organization, and the Commission's preliminary proposal sent to Parliament differs from the one sent to Geneva.
We believe it is necessary to assist Pakistan, and we understand that, exceptionally, commercial preferences may be granted, provided that due consideration is given to the sensitivities of the
the European industry, and particularly the textile sector, which in communities such as the Valencian Community in Spain is severely affected by the crisis.

However, this report has enabled us to verify that measures that can be taken within the framework of international trade likely cannot be addressed with the urgency these actions require, so we should learn from this for the future.
Assist Pakistan, yes, but not with the Commission's original proposal, as it fails to account for the impact on European industry regarding the implementation timelines for sensitive products.
Even delaying the implementation of these measures would put us beyond the deadline, as they do not align with the pending text intended for approval by the WTO.
Therefore, we consider it unacceptable to maintain this proposal, and we believe we will have to vote against it.
Mr. President, I would like to begin my intervention by congratulating everyone who has made it possible to reach an agreement.

It is an agreement to which we should all congratulate ourselves, as it affects the daily lives of all citizens; however, with the constructive spirit that characterizes me, I would like to make a few observations.
First of all, I would like to draw attention to the importance of providing accurate, relevant, but above all, understandable and consistent information.
for consumers regarding the components of textile products. It will be of no use to provide exhaustive information if citizens are then unable to interpret it.
We have achieved a positive step, necessary but not sufficient. We should move forward further and protect citizens who might become more vulnerable.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is essential to establish a European sizing labeling system based on body measurements, because the first thing we encounter when purchasing a garment is the size.
That is why we should be much more ambitious and strive for size harmonization.
Mr. President, three observations regarding the situation surrounding the cucumbers.
I am the grandson of a German and hold great affection for that country, but it is evident that the German authorities have acted in this case with haste and imprudence.

On the other hand, if we analyze the European response, we observe that the actions taken by the Food Safety Authority and the Commission have been muted, very subdued, and imprecise; they have failed to coordinate or provide leadership.
Third, the affected parties have the right to receive economic compensation promptly.
However, for the moment, I propose to this Parliament that we organize in Brussels or here in Strasbourg a major exhibition of European gastronomy precisely centered on the cucumber.
One must reclaim the lost honor—a phrase pleasing to the German writer Heinrich Böll—one must reclaim the lost honor of cucumber producers.
Europe is the appropriate setting for this.
Mr. President, first of all, it is obligatory to express sincere regret for the twenty-three fatal victims already suffered, twenty-two in Germany and one in Sweden.
And this crisis must be said to be the clearest evidence that the European Union must greatly improve its food alert system and internal coordination.
The involved Member States have demonstrated slowness, inefficiency, and ineffectiveness, and even now, after twenty-three deaths, we still do not know where the problem originates.

The European Commission must seriously reflect on the problems caused by its irresponsible actions or inaction during crises such as the current one, resulting in cases of economic ruin and destruction of jobs that were previously

unemployment, helplessness, and, of course, a public health crisis with extremely high health risks for the population. Because while you, from the Commission, echoed irresponsible statements by regional authorities

highly misinformed and rather irresponsible German entities, using the names and surnames of Spanish farms, meanwhile the population remained exposed without knowing it, believing they were avoiding the bacteria, so citizens were more vulnerable than ever to consuming something that could harm them.

Today, at the Agriculture Council taking place in Luxembourg at 2 p.m., the European Commission should propose to ministers that a portion of the 2 million euros remaining from the unutilized funds of the Common Agricultural Policy be used.
Need those 2 million euros to repair, as much as possible, the damage caused.

To this end, it is necessary to identify a legal framework that allows it—a legal framework that has already been found on other occasions, such as with mad cows or dioxins, and that should be found now.
Therefore, Commissioners, Ministers, today in Luxembourg you must rise to the occasion and meet your responsibilities.

German authorities, moreover, given the irresponsible accusations made, should undoubtedly support this economic solution from the outset and also finance, with their own funds, communication campaigns to restore consumer confidence lost in horticultural and fruit production.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, nothing we do here or in the European Union will bring back the dead, and I sincerely hope that what is being done will prevent further loss of life and put an end to the chain of suffering among those who are ill through no fault of their own.

We do not know the causes, surely, but we do know certain things that we must name as they are. There has been negligence in political actions, and there has been negligence in the German federal government. It cannot act as if Hamburg were a state on another continent.

The arrogance that Mrs. Merkel and her Health Minister display in frequently addressing other countries, if translated into diligent and effective coordination, would have prevented many of the events that have occurred.

And that negligence has had criminal consequences for a state that has been the Spanish state and for its most important sector, agriculture, leading to the ruin of thousands of families and small entrepreneurs and discrediting the economy for a long time.
I ask that you go to the Council today and request full compensation for those affected, since we cannot ask for lives back for the dead.
and that control systems be reviewed.
And that responsibility be demanded from Mrs. Merkel.
And that less arrogance and more diligence be requested of him.
And hold her responsible for damages she has caused.
It cannot ignore that Hamburg is within its state.
It cannot ignore its responsibilities.
It cannot hide behind a decentralized and federal state to inflict the damage it has caused.

A bit more humility, a bit better management, and a bit less conservatism would have gone a long way in preventing this crisis from getting out of control, sparing Spain and other southern farmers from enduring its painful consequences.
Please review the controls, please review the alert systems, and demand accountability, because in Spain we are asking you, Commissioner, as well as the Council and the German State, to do so.
Mr. President, we are facing a public health issue, an epidemic, as the Commissioner has said, and in response, we have sought scapegoats rather than addressing the root cause.

The system has failed; we have twenty-two dead, one affected, and we still do not know the cause three weeks later. And appealing to a precautionary principle without scientific basis—repeatedly, without scientific basis—we blame Spain.
Honorable deputies, the Spanish cucumbers were not contaminated.
They were contaminated, they were not contaminated; I repeat this because some contradictory versions have been heard here.
But to help you understand the significance of what these words have provoked, 300 jobs could be affected, along with a loss of confidence.
An epidemic knows no borders.

I agree with you, Commissioner; it knows no borders. We are demanding accountability for what has not been done, for what we must do immediately, because Germany is currently facing a risk situation, with the highest number of affected individuals, but Spain is not to blame.
We are demanding compensation.
What matters most and what unites us here today are the victims, because no one should feel as unprotected as European consumers feel today.
And it is out of respect for the victims that we need clarity and accountability.

And it is my responsibility to report to the German authorities who unjustly and hastily accused Spanish cucumbers, and specifically the cooperatives in Málaga and Almería, which were models of agricultural operations and will not recover from these accusations.
Thousands of unemployed families wonder why, why they need an answer, and above all, what their future will be, not only in Andalusia but throughout agricultural Europe.

Therefore, and trusting that experts will quickly find a solution, I demand that the Commission, when it speaks with the Council today, request immediate economic and moral measures for all producers—from Andalusia and from throughout Europe—who look to us with indignation, yet with hope.
Mr. President, instead of promoting research and European collaboration, German authorities have slandered and harmed cucumber producers and others.
And still, no one in Germany has resigned or offered reparations commensurate with the damage caused.
It is Germany that has a serious credibility problem.
It is unacceptable how Frau Merkel treats the countries of the south regarding agriculture and financial matters.
Someone said that Merkel was a disciple of Adenauer and Kohl.
None of that lacks credibility, Morán
Mr. President, it is no coincidence that Spain, Italy, Greece, Ireland, Portugal, or some other country share our reservations about readily accepting what is known as Eurovision.

The coincidence, the criticism stems from our condition as peripheral countries, which ultimately will be us who pay this unusual tax collected by the European Union member states that hold geographical centrality.
The reasoning underlying the eurovignette involves serious contradictions, and the supporting discourse grows increasingly fragile day by day.

Although one may accept as a starting point the rationality of internalizing external costs, in the end it does not hold that, in order to alleviate congestion, only large-tonnage vehicles should be penalized while tourists are left out, thus applying the principle that "the polluter pays" in an evident discriminatory manner.

On the other hand, ladies and gentlemen, the Eurovignette, which was envisioned as a source of funding for infrastructure, will ultimately allocate only 15% to the financing of trans-European networks, and not in a mandatory manner.

but as a mere recommendation as to manner and method, whereby the then-current Minister of Finance shall allocate the collected funds in the manner deemed most advantageous to the interests of his country, within the broad scope of infrastructure.
The final outcome will be yet another setback for freight transport by road, which today is the only means currently enabling the internal market.
This is the reality, ladies and gentlemen, a reality that is far harsher in the context of an economic crisis.

In this regard, the competitiveness of European products in global markets cannot afford experiments that might further deteriorate the already fragile state of the European economy through the imposition of additional costs.

And, of course, the food crisis, which recklessly has been shifted onto Spanish products, has resulted in unjust penalties and massive losses for the very same hauliers and farmers growing staple crops who are penalized by the eurovignette.
These are the reasons, Ladies and Gentlemen, why the Spanish delegation of the European Party will vote against this draft directive.

You, dear colleagues, will all understand that, assuming the chair of this session, I wish to express my sorrow and deep distress over the passing of Jorge Semprún, which occurred a few hours ago at the age of 87.

In Jorge Sanprún we honor a man who was imprisoned in the Buchenwald concentration and extermination camp, a fighter against the forces of Nazism in France, and against the Franco dictatorship in my country, Spain.

We also celebrate the distinguished writer committed to his cause, particularly a collaborator of our program The European Book of the Year, and we also celebrate the convinced Europeanist politician.

He also mourned his death as a colleague and friend, and I shall ask President Buzek to convey to his family and to the Government of Spain a message of deep sorrow and solidarity, which I am certain we all share here.
the creation of an economic governance space governed by principles of federalism adapted to the construction of Europe.
We cannot continue to turn a blind eye.
It is inconsistent to have a single currency while lacking the fiscal and financial instruments that such a currency demands.

Specifically, therefore, we need the issuance of European debt, a firm rejection of competition among the debt issuances of individual states, and the creation of a genuine European public treasury, the
Harmonization of fiscal policies that ensure equality among citizens, combat tax fraud, and help shape a progressive European social policy based on effective public services.
These ideas will be said to be elementary.
But they must be repeated and repeated, because nobody listens to them.
And precisely because they do not listen, those dark times spoken of by the poet Bertolt Brecht are upon us.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen, the current economic and financial crisis we are experiencing underscores the need for regional policy as the most effective instrument for reducing disparities in development among regions and Member States.
At the same time, we can reclaim the positive image of Europe that recently citizens have associated only with harsh adjustment plans and social cutbacks.

The future cohesion policy, a cornerstone of solidarity, the European project, and social justice, must take into account the following priorities: first, strengthen social cohesion and address the challenges posed by the 2020 Strategy; second, take into account the needs and characteristics

geographical conditions of the regions, especially the most disadvantaged ones. Third, maintain the principle of gradualism in the absorption of funds, so that regions do not abandon the objective of convergence, avoiding abrupt changes in the absorption of assistance, and fourth, promote a system
equitable.
Lastly, ladies and gentlemen, the authorities are implementing strict budgetary adjustment policies.
Therefore, Union support is necessary.
I hope the Council takes good note.

In defense of fundamental rights, I call on the responsible institutions to reconsider San Sebastián's designation as European Capital of Culture, as I believe it was selected on political grounds, which constitutes discrimination against the other candidate cities.

The president of the Tribunal stated that she was chosen to support the peace process, and as far as I know, in Spain there exists a terrorist organization called ETA, which to this day has not laid down its arms, and which two months ago succeeded in enabling its political wing, Bildu, to govern, among other municipalities, the city of San Sebastián.

I do not believe that from Europe we should give such a powerful platform to those who do not condemn terrorist violence and will instead seek to distribute blame equally between victims and murderers.
Where is the right to freedom and dignity?
Certainly not in San Sebastián, where comrades of ours, such as Mayor Oreja Iturgaiz, Salva, or even myself, must travel under escort.
I believe in the culture of life, not in the culture of the bullet.
Can there be true culture without freedom?
No and a thousand times no.
And when San Sebastián becomes a free city for all, let it reapply, and in the meantime, let them learn the culture of respect for those who think differently.
Because tomorrow they might deserve it, but today, with Batazuna and its heirs in the institutions, they do not deserve it.
Mr. President, first of all, I would like to thank the shadow rapporteurs for their fruitful collaboration in drafting this text.
And the same applies to the other fellow deputies of the Committee of this Parliament.
Sixty years ago, the European Coal and Steel Community was established. Nothing better to commemorate this event than to debate, today, the energy infrastructure in Europe.

Today, the European Union has clearly established the objectives of its common European internal market policy, promoting renewable energies as a means or instrument in the fight against climate change, energy security, and diversification of its energy sources.

Under the Lisbon Treaty, a dedicated title on energy has been established—Title XXI—where these commitments I have just mentioned are set forth, as well as the promotion of interconnection among energy networks.
Well then, the objective of the report we have prepared is aimed at correcting energy infrastructures that, in the opinion of the European Commission, are outdated and poorly interconnected.

Thus, for example, we observe a spectacular increase in electricity generation from wind power in the North Sea and the Baltic Sea, along with the immense potential for renewable energy in southern Europe.
Europe and in North Africa, with the challenges arising from large-scale electricity storage, the charging requirements for electric vehicles, and numerous other urgent issues.
All of this is impossible to address today unless we devise new networks and new connections, both within the European Union and with third countries.

Therefore, in the coming years, it is a matter of advancing in the identification of the necessary infrastructure, establishing the criteria that will allow the selection of projects of European interest.

b) eliminating energy islands  
c) ensuring the implementation of these projects within reasonable timeframes and devising solutions for the contentious issue of the
authorization and licensing procedures in the Member States, ensuring financing that attracts and drives private investment, without prejudice to the public support that may be necessary.
Energy efficiency and savings, as well as smart grids, are central components in the new framework incorporated into the report, because we have
It has been believed that reducing energy demand can decrease dependence on imports, with the consequent impact on infrastructure requirements.
The fundamental concern of this report from the outset has been the processing of permits and authorizations intended to allow the execution of the necessary works.
These, at present, are eternalized.
And they are hindered by local populations opposing the construction of towers or other facilities in their vicinity.

To resolve this conflict, early information to the public has been considered, along with establishing a warning system in the event that the authorities of a state fail to respond to a request within a reasonable timeframe.
We face ambitious goals for which the involvement of everyone is necessary.
In this regard, companies in the sector are essential, rightly calling for a stable regulatory framework—the best way to ensure financing for their efforts.
Until now, the European Union's energy policy, both toward its external borders and within its internal boundaries, has lacked a distinctly European driving force.
Europe must speak with one voice, a voice that is clearly audible and fully resonant within the vault of the European edifice, for it is from our energy fragility that we have
muster the strength to build an energy-solid and vigorous Europe, a worthy successor in this anniversary year to that great invention, the ECSC, our very cradle.
Mr. President, each time a compromise allows progress, we should congratulate ourselves; however, I do not believe the compromise we have reached is cause for a fit of happiness.
We must be prudent during the moments of happiness we experience.

It is true that we have made progress, particularly regarding food labeling, especially in terms of safety and message clarity; however, we have fallen short with regard to trans fats and, for example, the requirement to label them.
And what I do want to ask the Commission is not to exhaust the deadlines.

that in a continent so well-supplied as this, facing problems of overnutrition, heart disease, and obesity, we must accelerate and urgently address banning the addition of fats to foods, as Denmark has done, as Spain is doing, and as Australia has done
without democracy and without waiting any longer.
