It should also be added that our farmers must adhere to rigorous quality, environmental, and social standards that our Moroccan partners do not always respect.
To conclude, I will address the issue of Western Sahara.
According to the United Nations Charter, Western Sahara is a self-governing territory and the exploitation of its resources must directly benefit the local population.
Moreover, it constitutes a territory over which the United Nations does not recognize the sovereignty of the Kingdom of Morocco.
From the European Union, vigilance must be maintained regarding compliance with international law.

I support an agreement with Morocco, one that establishes rigorous control mechanisms from the European Union regarding quotas and prices, an agreement that combats dumping, does not exploit Moroccan farmers, and above all, upholds the dignity of our European farmers.
It is certainly not this agreement.

This year, the European Parliament's Petitions Committee requested that the European Commission amend the fruit and vegetable import pricing regime within the Union, a system that is facilitating fraudulent entry of produce from third countries, as acknowledged by the Union's own anti-fraud agency, OLAF.
Primarily they are Moroccan tomatoes.
The same request has been included in a statement signed last year by the Governments of Spain, France, and Italy.

This, this fraud, is resolved by amending the Regulation implementing the Common Market Organization for Agricultural Products, because aligning with the Customs Code does not solve the problem, Mr. Commissioner.

For several years I have argued within the People's Party for the need to halt new concessions to Morocco until the European Union acquires the necessary instruments to ensure compliance with the current agreement.
The European Commission argues that it is the Member States that must ensure control over imports.

However, what the Commission does not mention is that the current entry price regime prevents national customs authorities from properly tracing the prices declared by importers to verify that no irregularities exist.

They amend the Regulation implementing the Common Market Organization for agricultural products, and we will have resolved part of the problem, because otherwise the legal gap in European legislation prevents national authorities from carrying out their duties properly.

Madam President, from the European Parliament I wish to pay tribute today to all women. Although we have come a long way since March 1911, when women took to the streets demanding the right to vote and better working conditions, I believe that gender equality is still far from being achieved.
Today women can work without having to ask permission from anyone, but in many cases they still earn less than men for similar work, which is unacceptable.
We must also remember the women in rural areas, whose work goes unrecognized.
I believe another uncomfortable reality, yet one that must continue to be denounced, is violence against women.

This year alone, an excessive number of women have already been murdered, not counting the serious threats made by those who believe they have the right to inflict physical or psychological violence upon them.
It is time to say enough and to say it loudly and clearly.
From this Parliament, I reaffirm my commitment to the European Protection Order for Victims and to the Victims' Directive submitted by the Commission, which will be voted on, God willing, next June.
Mr. President, we must also say—and I would like to emphasize—that motherhood continues to be an obstacle to women's advancement, and I believe this trend should be changed.

Mr. President, I wish to send a message of hope, and together with many women, and with the growing support of men, we must advance along the path of equality, of opportunities, rights, and also of responsibilities.
We have come a long way, but there is much still to be done.
I must admit that I do not absolutely and deeply believe in quotas, but I must tell you that something needs to be done, and I agree.

With many of my colleagues, and of course with Mrs. Reding, in opening this debate and moving forward on this path so that women can regain the place they deserve—and to that end, she can count on my support.

Mr. President, at a time of economic crisis and budget cuts affecting public health—where, as in the community where I live, research centers such as the Príncipe Felipe, specialized in diabetes, are left without funding—this resolution could not come at a more appropriate moment; it is absolutely timely.

We need a common European strategy against diabetes, and we need a recommendation from the Council stating that Member States must increase research, including research involving stem cells, must coordinate their findings to optimize results, and must establish programs promoting healthy eating and lifestyles.

In short, we must place people's health above all else, and in this case, investigating, coordinating, and implementing national programs coordinated by the Commission and demanding results on diabetes is to ensure the quality of life of Europeans and to safeguard their very lives.

If we allocate more funds to this, some people will receive somewhat more, but undoubtedly those currently facing difficulties will enjoy a better quality of life, and we, as Member States, as the Council, and as the Commission, will be fulfilling one of our obligations—ensuring the health of our citizens.
Mr. President, among the many issues raised by this debate, I would like to highlight that of the emissions trading market, a market that undoubtedly began with flaws.
But today, the limitation of rights and the economic crisis have led to a drastic reduction in the price of carbon, meaning the price is so low that polluting has become cheap.
It is urgent, therefore, to reform the design of a policy regarding that market, as its deterioration threatens all green initiatives and the entire green policy of the European Union.
If emission rights are not valued, there is an incentive to increase emissions. Polluting industries will not invest in new techniques, and worse yet
The funding for important projects, such as those related to energy efficiency or carbon storage plants, will be jeopardized.
This is the risk.
It is our responsibility, that of the Commission and of Parliament, to avert it.

Another more institutional aspect is the balance of power between the Parliament and the Council.

Mr. Tavares has fought to ensure that the European Parliament maintains the new competences gained following the approval of the Lisbon Treaty.

UNHCR has made us all aware of the urgency of the report so that funds can be used as soon as possible, now that they are so greatly needed.
And finally, many thanks, Commissioner, and I hope we can also give you and your kind heart the satisfaction of advancing the asylum package next month.

The European social model, which we so proudly uphold, is not based solely on our pension, education, or healthcare systems, but also on a particular approach to balancing the relationship between employer and employee within companies.

The labor reform approved by the Spanish Government without dialogue or consensus breaks with that social model and moves us away from the objectives of the 2020 Strategy, both in terms of job creation and in terms of job quality.
A labor reform, by itself, does not create jobs; economic growth does.
What labor law does do is define under what conditions workers enter companies, under what conditions they remain in them, and under what conditions they leave them.

And with this reform, where 80 percent of workers will be left unprotected because they will not be covered by collective agreements, where they could be dismissed due to a decline in company revenues.

Because they may unilaterally alter their schedules, it is clear that workers will enter the labor market with greater precarity, remain in it with increased fear, and exit it with reduced compensation.

That is why this reform, which undermines the European social model and seeks the competitiveness of the Spanish economy solely in relation to workers' conditions, deserves a general strike, such as the one that must be called tomorrow in Spain to defend this social model.

The Government of Spain, in the short time it has been leading the executive branch, has adopted and will continue to adopt highly ambitious measures to ensure fiscal consolidation and lay the foundations for improved competitiveness, job creation, and growth.

We must all together save ourselves and Spain as well, without any doubt, and we must not allow any critical remarks from some or others; either all countries are capable of working together and contributing to growth and the new economy, or otherwise, it will naturally be difficult for us to move forward on the terms proposed by the Commission.
Now I understand. Do you want to make Mr. Rajoy, the President of the Government, the main character?
That seems fine to me; I wouldn't have any objection.

The protagonist of the crisis is the legacy inherited by Spain's socialist, communist, and green left, which has left behind the worst legacy ever left by any government—when they themselves received the best legacy any government has ever had.
Mr. President, this morning I read in a major German newspaper that Spain and Argentina are involved in a dispute concerning an oil company.

Mr. President, the Committee on Agriculture shares the ultimate objective of this Directive, which is to attempt to influence, through fiscal measures, the composition of our energy mixes; however, we are facing a very complex and difficult debate.

We are full of tensions in economic, social, and environmental terms, and it is important to preserve those balances that enable us to make greater progress on environmental issues, yes, but without harming us economically or creating deeper social crises than the ones we are already experiencing.
This is the case with agriculture.

As everyone knows, energy costs are increasingly representing a major portion of the fixed costs in our agricultural operations, at a time when we are also engaged in a debate that combines crises in raw material markets with expectations of financial assistance, alongside growing health and environmental requirements that are narrowing profit margins.
That is why we have essentially requested two things.

The first, the maintenance of zero-rate deductions in the energy component, now linked to the drafting and implementation of energy efficiency and savings strategies in rural areas, and the incorporation of
agriculture within the sectors considered at risk of carbon leakage, in order to also qualify for the deductions allowed in the CO2 emissions threshold.
That is, a more environmentally demanding policy, yet one that preserves those balances that make our rural areas viable, especially during a period of change and uncertainty within them.
We are experiencing a major controversy that inevitably references the concepts of austerity and growth.
If one hears the noise being generated, it would seem that the idea of growth is a brilliant invention of these days.
Nothing could be further from the truth.
In Europe, the Pact has been in effect since 1997, precisely known as the Stability and Growth Pact.

Therefore, a large part of the policies implemented by European institutions, such as regional cohesion and research policies, are growth-oriented policies aimed at promoting development and the well-being of citizens.

Therefore, and addressing the Commission and those members of this Parliament who have the greatest capacity to be heard, we must not feel any sense of inferiority in participating in that debate, for we can face it with our heads held high.
This is my minority opinion among those not enrolled.

Being an ambitious proposal, it is insufficient to protect vulnerable consumers, because their vulnerability arises precisely from their lack of assertiveness and understanding of the

The Commission once again appears before this Parliament, this Chamber, as if it were a political commentator, as if it were just another analyst, rather than the true government of the Union, which is what it is and which is precisely what we need.

Mr. Commissioner, to govern is to set priorities, to govern is to set priorities, to take the initiative, and furthermore, with the aim of achieving some goal, some objective—and moreover, the Commission is not doing so.

Governance is not merely carrying out a simple reallocation of funds, nor even doing so and announcing it merely to clean one's image in the eyes of public opinion; rather, it is advancing an ambitious, sustainable, and swift strategy to eradicate youth unemployment.

Governance does not mean resigning oneself to a youth unemployment rate above 20 percent and being satisfied with a pilot project worth four million euros, which is absurd in light of the situation and the scale of the problem we face.

To govern without yielding to the pressures from the Board of Commissioners or from other member states of the Council, who strive to convince us that from this financial crisis, this new wave of recession, we can only emerge by surrendering wages, labor rights, or compensation—that we are left with no future but one of precariousness as a generation

They are Sweden, Finland, and Denmark, precisely characterized by a model of harmonious labor relations and also by a model of guaranteed labor rights.
Mr. President, employment is much more than a civic right; it is a tool for connecting with the system, a tool for social integration.

Because, Mr. Commissioner, when it has been necessary to interpret the Treaties loosely in order to establish, for example, the Financial Stability Facility, you have done so, and when the Treaties have had to be amended to fulfill or satisfy the delusions of some Member State regarding austerity, we have done that as well.
Therefore, what is it that I want to tell you?
There, for example, is the Youth Employment Guarantee; there's no need to explain it to you.

Fight to increase the budget, and if necessary, use the European Social Fund as a tool; design a co-financed and solidarity-based implementation mechanism that best supports those with the highest unemployment rates; push for further changes to this social preference policy in favor of countries faring the worst, and remember that behind every figure is a person suffering.
Then, will he think, as I do, that what matters least is whose competence it is, because for the body of citizens, especially...

Mr. President, I already supported Mrs. Nadelcheva's report when I had the opportunity to accompany her on the mission to the Congo together with other parliamentarians, and indeed, I have seen few peoples with a more evident will to make democracy work and to participate in it.
But a people, in order to believe in democracy, must also believe in their State and their Government, and this cannot be achieved through electoral support or external backing alone.
They also require a commitment to fighting corruption and ending the indecent paradox of living in poverty in one of the richest countries in the world in terms of resources.

And it must be said and denounced that a part of the violence they endure is imported—or at least encouraged—by those multinational corporations without scruples, which, from Europe or other continents or states, are capable of participating in corruption while remaining indifferent to fostering conflicts over these resources in order to gain favor with those who govern certain territories.

Therefore, Ms. Ashton, although it is not entirely within your purview, I do assert that a greater demand for traceability and transparency from European multinationals and others from other continents collaborating there would be just as beneficial for the development of that people as the assistance we might provide at the electoral and technical levels.

Madam President, since I entered this Parliament, I have not ceased to denounce female genital mutilation.
That silent and accepted crime endured by millions of women and girls around the world—seventy million, I repeat, seventy million.

An unbearable figure for anyone who pauses to examine the terrible consequences of these practices, which are concealed behind the guise of psychological, health-related, and religious reasons—all of them false.

The truth is that the procedure, predominantly performed on girls aged fourteen and even as young as one year old, can lead to death from hemorrhagic shock, and in the best-case scenario, cause severe psychological sequelae due to trauma.
From Europe, we must fight with all our strength to eradicate this violation of human rights, which undermines the right to equal opportunities and endangers health.
Your Lordships, this is abuse, cruelty, torture inflicted upon girls, and such degrading treatment also exists in Europe.
To this end, we must activate all preventive mechanisms available within our legal system, and I urge our judges to act in order to prevent female genital mutilation.
of girls in Europe, punishing those who carry them out, so that the law in Europe speaks with one voice when combating mutilation on our soil.
Finally, I urge civil society and NGOs to actively engage in education and prevention campaigns.
There is still a long way to go, but many of us will not stop because we owe it to seventy million women who have been mutilated.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, I am fully aware that one of the reasons we approved the dairy package was precisely to gradually adapt the sector toward a market without milk quotas by 2015.

The required fuels will be more expensive, and therefore economic assistance for business owners is anticipated, which is very reasonable.
However, some colleagues have attempted to extend the requirements for new fuels to all seas of the European Union.

We would face the paradox that an environmental problem affecting some countries within the Union would be addressed by creating a major economic problem in the Mediterranean— a region that does not have that particular environmental issue, but does face other types of environmental problems whose costs are not intended to be imposed on the rest of the countries.
The Treaty is very clear.
Sensitive areas must be protected.
However, not all areas are equally sensitive.

Mr. President, I would like to once again thank everyone who has made it possible for this Directive to become a reality—the Secretariat, the Commissioner, the rapporteur, all those I may have forgotten, and all my colleagues who have stood with us.
I want to say to the victims, to all of them, absolutely all of them, without exception, that to us they and their families have a face, have a name, are real, and precisely in their name.

Today we will work to ensure that this Directive does not remain merely纸上谈兵, but that, with the commitment and support of Commissioner Reding—whom I absolutely do not doubt— it achieves swift and effective implementation, which is what we all expect and desire.
citizen.

I believe there are different groups of victims who would have liked and still wish for their own directive, but I will tell you that the directive we will vote on tomorrow does not overlook any victim, and therefore does not overlook them either.
And I am certain that this is a first and significant step, one that will be followed by many others that will open those doors to justice which have remained closed too long for victims.
When they ask me in my country what it is that I do in Europe, I will be able to say with great pride that I am legislating in favor of victims.
And that work deeply honors me personally and gives me great strength to continue working.

I am confident that with the support and commitment not only of those of us working here in Europe, but also of our colleagues in our respective countries, we will, together—with the assistance of Your Lordship—finally free the victims from that unjust oblivion.

Mr. President, my colleague Turmes has done an outstanding job, as advancing a regulation of this nature required the courage of this rapporteur, who has skillfully neutralized the many obstacles so cleverly put in place.
It has been worthwhile because efficiency in energy use and its conservation are requirements of any energy policy worthy of the name.

We should commend significant measures such as the requirement for the renovation of public buildings, the imposition of an accumulated energy savings percentage on energy companies, as well as mandatory audits for large private enterprises.
Moreover, will the work of Turmes and the other rapporteurs be of no avail if it is not accompanied by determined action from the Member States?
An action that, in turn, must be accompanied by a firm commitment to these objectives from the regions, municipalities, and European citizens.
I am interested in highlighting the involvement of these latter individuals.
For now, what is observed is much frivolity among authorities and among citizens.
Don't we, dear colleagues, see people seated on heated street terraces during Europe's harsh winter?
Friend Turness, we must not spare efforts in order to save energy.
We must commend the rapporteur, Mrs. Aira García, for the work she has carried out, and congratulate ourselves on the agreement reached between the Parliament and the Council of Ministers.
It is a pity that the Council is not with us this evening.
Mr. President, the new quality framework for agricultural products represents a simplification of the system and a streamlining of existing authorization procedures, which is good news.

Moreover, it is a significant achievement that the obligation to use the Community logo for designations of origin and protected geographical indications has been included, as this enhances the visibility of European productions.
It also seems noteworthy that provisions protecting appeals on quality are strengthened in bilateral agreements with third countries and a legal basis is established for
Defend the logo of the European Union in those third countries, as I sincerely believe that protecting European products in third countries must be an unyielding objective.

It is a pity that during the negotiation this package has been somewhat weakened, and again I say it is regrettable that the Council is not here to hear it, because the original quality package that was proposed
The Commission has somewhat neglected the commercial standards and quality management by the quality designation bodies, detached from the agreement and, as I say, leaving it somewhat empty.
I believe we must regret the rigidity shown by the Council on these two matters, refusing to hear the Parliament's positions, which are reasonable.
The management of supply by appellations of origin should have been defended, and it will certainly be defended at the WTO.

Mr. President, the trade agreement with Central America, Colombia, and Peru is an important accord, and the safeguard clause aims, through the amendments we have proposed, to ensure it is simple, swift, and provides adequate guarantees.

We have presented amendments to protect European production, in accordance with the Treaties, and we establish security mechanisms both for European production and for production in those countries where the Agreement is implemented.
Protection of the outermost regions is necessary, as they are not diversified in cultivation, due to economic factors, as they affect a large number
jobs, more than 10 direct jobs and 35 indirect ones in Spain, in a population heavily affected by unemployment and its specific circumstances.
For example, the Canary Islands banana has a high degree of specificity in agriculture, culture, and gastronomy.

But it is also necessary there, in the regions where we will establish the agreement—Central America, Colombia, and Peru—because trade is fundamental to development, and development entails labor rights, sustainability, and benefits for the producer.

Mr. President, the rapporteur's work is very good, but I would like to take this opportunity to say here, in this setting, that our prose is highly confusing, as anyone who approaches European legislation can readily observe.
Let us set aside what Puffendorf already taught, namely, that general laws should be few and their meaning must be correctly understood.
For them, those who promulgate it must use as much clarity as possible.
And Montesquieu always insisted that legal language must be precise and concise.
That is, we must avoid esoteric jargon, filled with neologisms and unjustified linguistic borrowings, because it creates a malignant network where traps flourish and arbitrariness takes root.
We deal with very complex issues in this Parliament, but it is our duty to convey them to citizens in a comprehensible and accessible manner.
It is common for legal professionals themselves—judges, lawyers—to become frustrated when confronted with our texts.
Good prose, dear colleagues, is the legislator's courtesy and a way of demonstrating respect for the citizen.
Honorable deputy, I have just spent the time—limited as it was—insulting and discrediting you from the most complete ignorance.
Ignorance. Do you know the Spanish Constitution?

Because what the Vice President, Mr. Vidal Cuadrás, has said is nothing more than the application of what is stated in the Spanish Constitution. Everything else consists of singular personal opinions that are entirely irrelevant, which I may respect but which I disdain.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, there exists an old principle that has moved from the legal sphere into the political realm: pacta sunt servanda, meaning agreements are made to be kept.
By this I mean the significant measures adopted by the European Council in June to address the crisis affecting us.

Since the adoption of these agreements, market pressures eased and these were positively received, as they may break the vicious cycle between banks and sovereign debt, as well as the plans to advance toward a genuine economic and monetary union and the commitment to defend the euro in an irreversible manner.
The European Council, held last week, contains, Mr. and Madam Members, undeniable positive aspects. The conclusions regarding progress toward banking union are satisfactory.
It is also positive that Agreement A27 provides for the establishment of the banking supervisor. Finally, it is appropriate that the implementation be entrusted to the eurozone's finance ministers.
But they will allow him to make a critical assessment of the biased interpretations that have been unilaterally made of it.

Your Honors, it is unacceptable to make particular interpretations that may distort the mandate granted to the ministers of economy, nor do we approve of matters being reported as agreements when they were not even discussed.
It is urgent to move toward a single supervisory mechanism, which must cover all financial institutions.
A common deposit guarantee scheme and a crisis resolution mechanism are also required.
The survival of the euro is linked to the urgent creation of an effective banking union.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner, the advantages of the internal market are undeniable—increased competition, and greater availability of products and services.
services, creation of new jobs, more affordable prices for consumers, and greater protection—still insufficient, and we cannot afford complacency.
However, cultural, linguistic, protectionist, and bureaucratic barriers prevent citizens and businesses from fully benefiting from the single market.
Half a year ago we celebrated the launch of the twelve priorities aimed at stimulating growth and reinforcing citizens' confidence in the market.
Unfortunately, an agreement has not yet been reached on most of the proposals, as the Commission itself acknowledged in last week's communication.

Ladies and Gentlemen, Commissioner, the urgency of the crisis compels us to be ambitious, to go much further, and to reach agreements as soon as possible to drive growth, employment, and confidence among citizens and businesses in the single market.
Mr. President, I too would like to congratulate the rapporteur and the other colleagues who have worked on this reform very adequately.

The financial crisis has intensified due to the existence of complex and obscure tradable assets linked to derivative instruments and off-exchange transactions, all aimed at achieving high returns in a short period of time.
It is necessary to bring financial operations into a transparent market to ensure proper price formation and the competitiveness of tradable products.
But above all, it is essential that the information provided be understandable to investors, who must have full awareness in order to freely consent to the risks they undertake.
Transparency is of no use as a brilliant flash of light if the confusion in the design of tradable products blinds citizens' understanding.

Mr. President, I appreciate the report by Mrs. Thyssen, because I believe that anything which strengthens the role and authority of this Parliament in political debate is moving in the right direction.
Too often we hear accusations leveled against European institutions regarding their limited democratic quality.
It is a statement made casually; this is not the occasion to enter into this debate.

Yes, to emphasize that this Parliament possesses the same legitimacy as any national parliament, and that, indeed, in some respects, it may be governed by more transparent rules and also afford its members greater freedom.
Therefore, all economic measures must pass through the filter represented by this Parliament before their final approval, and subsequently during the examination of their implementation and outcomes.
the group of experts designated to verify in the Member States compliance with the conditions imposed by the Community authorities and the representatives of the Member States
Stability mechanisms must be present in this Chamber so that we deputies can have access to comprehensive and firsthand information.
Mr. President, 5.3% of the active population works in the automotive sector.
The figures speak for themselves.
The decline in sales in the European market, combined with corporate strategy, means that, in general terms, companies are undergoing externalization and restructuring
There is a crisis of overcapacity currently affecting ten assembly plants and potentially up to one hundred thousand workers.

Unfortunately, there are few cases of restructuring with a long-term vision; generally, a short-term perspective prevails, in which the main focus is gaining competitiveness through labor adjustments.

I must point out that on the very same day Ford communicated the shutdown of the GEN plant, it also informed the Valencia Ford facility that its production would be transferred to Germany, in the same corporate announcement.

Therefore, we believe this is a general issue for Europe, and we consider it essential to establish a legal framework at the European level that anticipates changes and underscores the importance of social dialogue.
Social dialogue is fundamental.
Mr. President, today we are debating four reports that represent a revitalization of our common policy on security and defense.

It is a clear indication of the conviction that our security and defense have a distinct European dimension, in contrast to the minority factions and euro-skeptical voices that also prevail in this Chamber.
Mr. Danjan's report, which I would like to thank for his spirit of seeking consensus throughout its preparation, contains significant contributions from the Socialist Group.
among others, references to effective multilateralism, the responsibility to protect principle, or the concept of human security.
And we would like to see these aspects included in the possible review of the European defense strategy.
We have also pointed out that a strong common security and defense policy is of interest to our transatlantic alliance as well.
I must highlight another challenge that is acquiring significant dimensions for our global security.
It is the one on cybersecurity and cyberdefence, a report that we also discussed today, and whose recommendations we hope will be included in the new European cybersecurity strategy.
In a context of severe economic and financial crisis, in which Member States are being forced to cut their defense budgets, is precisely when European projects are most needed.
That is why it is more important than ever to strengthen the role of the European Defence Agency, which is currently facing significant criticism.
I conclude by stating that we can relaunch European security and defense, and do so with the project proposed by former President von der Leyen for the December 2013 Council.
Thank you very much to the four speakers.
Mr. President, Ladies and Gentlemen, once again the defense of the general interests, championed by the Commission and Parliament, confronts national self-interests that prevail in the Council.
Given the prior acceptance of the legitimacy of both positions, reaching a balance is inexcusable.
The relevance of the upcoming financial framework is unquestionable.
The Spanish delegation of the PPE and I, on its behalf, reiterate that, in our view, the Presidency's proposal is unacceptable.
A cut of 75 million euros from the already inevitably meager proposal by the European Commission proves sterile and useless.
And it is not only that the new cut directly to agricultural and cohesion policies goes beyond the red line that Spain has drawn since the beginning of the negotiations.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is no longer the time to debate a supposed dichotomy between austerity and growth.
Some of us are making proven efforts and sacrifices to correct the deficit.
But Spain, like many other countries, needs to grow, increase economic activity, and create jobs.
And with this budget, one gets nowhere; on the contrary, it acts as a missile aimed at the waterline.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is not possible to achieve more Europe with fewer resources, and especially not if what exists is distributed unfairly.

If that is the case, Spain will oppose a financial framework for the European Union that amounts to the outright elimination of growth prospects and, above all, any trace of solidarity, which is precisely the direction the European Council is taking.
Fortunately, this is not the path indicated by the Commission, to which we must express our gratitude for its efforts in charting a different route.

And at this point, in the need for unity, in the need for shared efforts, I would like to thank Mr. Barroso for his firm statement the other day in Cádiz, offering a clear interpretation of the Treaties regarding a hypothetical secessionist scenario within the Union.

He made it clear, unequivocally, as guardian of the Treaties, that if a part of a Member State separates as an independent State, it will be regarded as a third country and, therefore, will not be subject to the provisions or effects of the Treaty itself.

I hope, Mr. Vice President, in the absence of the Mr. President, that this may be taken up and that this safe and consistent doctrine of the Commission may continue to remain alive as assurance for all.
